# AI Case Database Setup

## Overview
- **Date**: 2026-03-13 18:45:49
- **Conversation ID**: `69b4a196-20a4-8326-8667-d9c52e92d575`
- **Source**: `Chat-Export-28513-2026/conversations-004.json`

## Chat History

### User
> i want to get this all in some database connected to ai with views. for quick easy retrieval and review for full case context.

### Assistant
> I’m mapping what you already have into something queryable instead of starting from scratch. The good news: you’re not far off — you already have the right buckets, just not a clean relational spine yet.

### Assistant
> Yes. And bluntly: **you should stop treating this like a folder problem and turn it into a case graph with a relational spine**.
> 
> You already have pieces of that spine:
> - a Postgres-oriented admin setup with scripts applying schema migrations and seeding `phases`, `lanes`, `tasks`, and `docs` into a litigation database, which means the repo was already moving toward a structured database model. fileciteturn1file10
> - a schema audit that expects formal IDs for phases, lanes, tasks, docs, events, claims, filings, and exhibits, which is exactly the right instinct for AI retrieval. fileciteturn1file4
> - clean content buckets for case summary, events, violations, damages, actors, disputes, and vault evidence. fileciteturn0file46 fileciteturn0file53 fileciteturn0file48 fileciteturn0file49 fileciteturn0file45 fileciteturn0file51 fileciteturn0file55
> 
> So the move is not “pick a database.”  
> The move is: **formalize one source of truth and make AI query that, not your raw folders.**
> 
> ## Best setup
> 
> Use:
> 
> **Postgres + pgvector + lightweight app layer**
> 
> Not Notion as the source of truth. Not Capacities as the source of truth. Not a giant spreadsheet as the source of truth.
> 
> Why:
> - You need **structured joins** across events, filings, actors, violations, damages, and exhibits.
> - You need **AI retrieval** over narrative text and PDFs.
> - You need **views** for human review: timeline, issue map, evidence matrix, damages ledger, deadline board.
> - You need **stable IDs** so one fact can point to many things without drift.
> 
> ## What the database should actually contain
> 
> Not just `phases/lanes/tasks/docs`. That’s too skinny for this case.
> 
> You need these core tables:
> 
> ### 1. `cases`
> One row for the FCFCU matter.
> 
> Fields:
> - `case_id`
> - `case_name`
> - `cause_number`
> - `court`
> - `status`
> - `summary`
> 
> You already have this info in the case summary. fileciteturn0file46
> 
> ### 2. `actors`
> People, institutions, agencies, counsel, sheriff, bureaus, witnesses.
> 
> You already started this in `07_ACTORS`, though some roles are misassigned and need cleanup. For example, Cody is labeled `creditor` and FCFCU is labeled `opposing counsel`, which is structurally wrong and will poison retrieval if you leave it like that. fileciteturn0file45
> 
> Fix actor typing to:
> - party
> - counsel
> - court
> - agency
> - utility
> - witness
> - contact
> - third_party
> 
> ### 3. `events`
> This is the timeline backbone.
> 
> Fields:
> - `event_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `event_date`
> - `event_time`
> - `title`
> - `event_type`
> - `summary`
> - `facts`
> - `legal_observations`
> - `ada_notes`
> - `impact`
> - `source_confidence`
> 
> Your repo already contains dated event records like mortgage execution, hardship notice delivery, removal-day events, and post-removal harm logs. fileciteturn0file53 fileciteturn1file18
> 
> ### 4. `documents`
> Every filing, letter, PDF, memo, dispute, strategy doc, transcript, image, exhibit candidate.
> 
> Fields:
> - `doc_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `title`
> - `doc_type`
> - `file_path`
> - `file_hash`
> - `source_folder`
> - `date_created`
> - `date_filed`
> - `author_actor_id`
> - `ocr_text`
> - `clean_text`
> - `privilege_level`
> - `is_exhibit_candidate`
> 
> You already have a huge document universe in `04_DOCUMENTS`, `05_DISPUTES`, and `99_VAULT`. fileciteturn0file54 fileciteturn0file51 fileciteturn0file55
> 
> ### 5. `filings`
> Court docket items specifically.
> 
> Fields:
> - `filing_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `docket_date`
> - `title`
> - `filing_type`
> - `party_filing`
> - `related_doc_id`
> - `result`
> - `court_action`
> - `docket_sequence`
> 
> You already have a docket summary mapping filings chronologically, including complaint, summons, service returns, default judgment, sheriff sale, plaintiff bid, decree returned, writ filings, motion to vacate, and denial order. fileciteturn0file55
> 
> ### 6. `violations`
> This is one of your strongest lanes and should be first-class.
> 
> Fields:
> - `violation_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `title`
> - `theory_cluster`
> - `primary_statute`
> - `risk_level`
> - `violation_date_start`
> - `violation_date_end`
> - `summary`
> - `why_it_matters`
> - `status`
> 
> You already maintain a violation inventory with categories like due process, ADA, FCRA, tort, fair housing, and spoliation, plus specific IDs tied to jurisdictional defects. fileciteturn0file48
> 
> ### 7. `damages`
> Not just total numbers. Atomic loss records.
> 
> Fields:
> - `damage_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `damage_type`
> - `date_incurred`
> - `amount`
> - `estimated_amount`
> - `description`
> - `causal_event_id`
> - `causal_violation_id`
> - `supporting_doc_id`
> 
> You already have a valuation model with settlement bands and a damages inventory including forced relocation, hotel costs, utility cost shift, lost tax-season income, and other harm categories. fileciteturn0file49
> 
> ### 8. `deadlines`
> This is your action clock.
> 
> Fields:
> - `deadline_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `title`
> - `due_date`
> - `deadline_type`
> - `status`
> - `trigger_event_id`
> - `related_phase`
> - `notes`
> 
> You already have dispute/deadline records like regulator follow-up and settlement response windows. fileciteturn0file51
> 
> ### 9. junction tables
> This is where the case becomes useful.
> 
> You need:
> - `event_documents`
> - `event_actors`
> - `event_violations`
> - `document_actors`
> - `document_violations`
> - `violation_damages`
> - `filing_documents`
> - `filing_events`
> 
> Without these, you’ll just rebuild folder chaos inside SQL.
> 
> ## AI layer
> 
> This is the part people screw up.
> 
> Do **not** just dump files into a vector DB and call it “AI.” That gives you fuzzy vibes, not litigation retrieval.
> 
> Use a **hybrid model**:
> 
> ### Structured retrieval
> For things like:
> - “show every event tied to lack of service”
> - “what filings mention satisfaction”
> - “what damages connect to post-sale utility billing”
> - “what documents support V-FCFCU-2025-060”
> 
> That comes from SQL.
> 
> ### Semantic retrieval
> For things like:
> - “find the strongest admissions that FCFCU knew about disability”
> - “surface all text relating to access denial after removal”
> - “find contradictions around satisfaction vs delinquency reporting”
> 
> That comes from embeddings on:
> - document text
> - event summaries
> - violation descriptions
> - filing abstracts
> 
> The hybrid answer is what you want: **SQL first, embeddings second**.
> 
> ## Views you actually need
> 
> These are the human-facing views:
> 
> ### 1. Master Timeline View
> Date-sorted, filterable by:
> - event type
> - actor
> - lane
> - theory cluster
> - document attached
> 
> ### 2. Docket View
> Court filings only, with:
> - filing date
> - docket title
> - linked PDF
> - linked events
> - linked violations
> 
> ### 3. Violation Matrix
> Each violation with:
> - statute
> - risk
> - supporting events
> - supporting docs
> - supporting damages
> - status of proof
> 
> ### 4. Evidence Review View
> Every document with:
> - hash
> - OCR status
> - privilege flag
> - exhibit candidate
> - linked events/issues
> 
> ### 5. Damages Ledger
> Sortable by:
> - type
> - amount
> - confidence
> - proof attached
> - linked theory
> 
> ### 6. Actor Map
> Each actor with:
> - role
> - linked communications
> - linked filings
> - linked events
> - linked exposure
> 
> ### 7. Hearing Prep View
> A filtered view:
> - unresolved contradictions
> - key admissions
> - timeline anchors
> - witness targets
> - open proof gaps
> 
> ## Hard truth about your current state
> 
> Right now the repo is **conceptually strong but structurally inconsistent**.
> 
> Main problems:
> - duplicate/parallel systems
> - mixed naming conventions
> - some malformed IDs
> - some files acting like notes, some like records, some like drafts
> - PDFs sitting in vault without normalized OCR/text extraction
> - actor roles are not reliably typed
> - some data is embedded in markdown bodies instead of fields
> 
> The schema audit already hints at that drift problem. fileciteturn1file4  
> And `08_ENTITIES` literally says entities are still being managed elsewhere and that folder is basically a placeholder. fileciteturn0file44
> 
> So no, you do **not** need another workspace.  
> You need to **normalize the one you already have**.
> 
> ## Best practical stack for you
> 
> Given your style and this case:
> 
> - **Postgres** = source of truth
> - **pgvector** = embeddings
> - **Python ingestion scripts** = parse markdown/YAML/PDF metadata into tables
> - **Simple internal UI** = maybe Streamlit, Retool, or a lightweight React/Supabase-style admin
> - **Filesystem remains archive/evidence store**
> - **DB becomes the operational brain**
> 
> That means:
> - files stay in Drive/repo/vault
> - database stores metadata, text, relationships, hashes, and retrieval indexes
> - AI queries the DB + text chunks, not random folders
> 
> ## Order of operations
> 
> Do this in this order:
> 
> ### Phase 1 — Lock schema
> Define the final tables and ID rules.
> 
> ### Phase 2 — Ingest existing structured content
> Ingest:
> - case summary
> - actors
> - events
> - violations
> - damages
> - disputes/deadlines
> - filings/docket summary
> 
> ### Phase 3 — Ingest vault documents
> Hash, OCR/text extract, classify, and link PDFs.
> 
> ### Phase 4 — Build views
> Timeline, docket, violations, damages, evidence, hearing-prep.
> 
> ### Phase 5 — Add AI retrieval
> Hybrid SQL + embedding search.
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> **Use Postgres as the case operating system.**
> Your folders become storage.
> Your markdown becomes source material.
> Your database becomes truth.
> Your AI becomes a query layer over that truth.
> 
> That is the cleanest path to:
> - fast retrieval
> - full case context
> - better hearing prep
> - less self-sabotage
> - cleaner attorney intake
> - actual leverage
> 
> The wrong move would be shoving this into a pretty no-code tool first and hoping structure appears later. It won’t.
> 
> The right move is to define the schema once, ingest everything, and then put whatever UI you want on top.
> 
> I can turn this into the actual **table schema + field list + view map + ingestion plan** next.

### User
> i added a graphiti plus nat geo 4j combo to my qiarchive in railway.

### Assistant
> I’m checking the exact stack names first because one of those looks like a typo, and I don’t want to give you bad architecture advice off a wrong assumption. Then I’ll tell you whether this actually helps your case system or just adds shiny complexity.

### Assistant
> Good. And yes, I’m assuming you meant **Graphiti + Neo4j**, not “nat geo 4j.” Railway actually has a **Graphiti + Neo4j** template for time-aware AI memory, and Graphiti is built for extracting entities, relationships, episodes, and temporal context into a graph-backed memory layer. It commonly runs with Neo4j as the graph backend. citeturn873562search0turn873562search10
> 
> That said, here’s the real answer:
> 
> ## This is useful — but only if you use it for the right layer
> 
> For your case system, **Graphiti/Neo4j should not become the primary source of truth**.
> 
> Use it as:
> - **relationship memory**
> - **entity linking**
> - **cross-document context**
> - **natural-language exploration of the case**
> 
> Do **not** use it as:
> - the sole case database
> - the official damages ledger
> - the authoritative docket table
> - the only place deadlines or filing status live
> 
> Why: Graphiti is great for evolving knowledge graphs and hybrid semantic + graph search, but your litigation workflow still needs hard structured tables for dates, filings, damages, and proof status. Railway’s own template describes it as a time-aware knowledge graph for AI memory and hybrid search, which is exactly the right secondary role here. citeturn873562search0turn873562search2
> 
> ## The right architecture for you
> 
> Use a **two-layer model**:
> 
> ### Layer 1: relational spine
> This is your clean case DB:
> - cases
> - actors
> - events
> - documents
> - filings
> - violations
> - damages
> - deadlines
> - junction tables
> 
> This is where truth lives.
> 
> ### Layer 2: graph memory
> This is where Graphiti/Neo4j helps:
> - “show me every connection between FCFCU, service defects, ADA notice, and the writ”
> - “what entities recur across the removal timeline and credit-report issues”
> - “what facts cluster around post-judgment conduct”
> - “surface related episodes even when wording differs”
> 
> That is where graph wins.
> 
> ## What Graphiti is especially good for in your case
> 
> You have a case full of:
> - repeated actors
> - repeated institutions
> - timeline dependencies
> - related-but-not-identical facts
> - contradictions across documents
> - evolving theories of harm
> 
> That is graph territory.
> 
> Useful nodes:
> - people
> - organizations
> - agencies
> - addresses
> - accounts
> - docket items
> - documents
> - events
> - legal theories
> - statutes
> - damage categories
> 
> Useful edges:
> - `FILED_BY`
> - `REFERENCES`
> - `SERVED_AT`
> - `AFFECTED`
> - `CONTRADICTS`
> - `SUPPORTS`
> - `LED_TO`
> - `OCCURRED_BEFORE`
> - `OCCURRED_AFTER`
> - `REPORTED_TO`
> - `RELATES_TO_VIOLATION`
> - `CAUSED_DAMAGE`
> 
> ## What not to do
> 
> Do not start dumping every raw file into Graphiti with no normalization. That gets messy fast.
> 
> Bad path:
> - throw PDFs in
> - auto-extract whatever
> - trust the graph as truth
> - build views directly from fuzzy nodes
> 
> That will become a hallucination-friendly spaghetti ball.
> 
> ## What to do instead
> 
> ### Immediate best move
> Keep your existing archive and build a clean ingestion pipeline:
> 
> 1. **Normalize the core records first**
>    - actors
>    - events
>    - filings
>    - violations
>    - damages
>    - documents
> 
> 2. **Assign stable IDs**
>    - `ACT-...`
>    - `EVT-...`
>    - `DOC-...`
>    - `FIL-...`
>    - `VIO-...`
>    - `DAM-...`
> 
> 3. **Push normalized records into relational DB**
>    - Postgres still needs to exist
> 
> 4. **Mirror high-value records into Graphiti**
>    - event summaries
>    - document abstracts
>    - actor/entity mentions
>    - violation summaries
>    - timeline relationships
> 
> 5. **Build views from SQL first**
>    - timeline
>    - docket
>    - evidence matrix
>    - damages ledger
>    - hearing prep
> 
> 6. **Use graph queries as enrichment**
>    - related context
>    - hidden link discovery
>    - semantic recall
>    - contradiction clustering
> 
> ## Straight verdict
> 
> **Adding Graphiti + Neo4j was not a bad move.**
> It was probably a **smart move** for your long-term AI retrieval layer.
> 
> But if you let it replace a proper case database, that will be a mistake.
> 
> Your stack should be:
> 
> - **QiArchive files** = storage/archive
> - **Postgres** = source of truth
> - **Graphiti + Neo4j** = case memory + relationship engine
> - **AI views/UI** = retrieval and review layer
> 
> That’s the clean architecture.
> 
> ## Best next step
> 
> You need a concrete schema that says:
> 
> - what lives in Postgres
> - what gets mirrored into Graphiti
> - what IDs and edge types you’ll use
> - what views the UI should expose
> 
> I can draft that as a **full case data architecture spec** for QiArchive next: tables, graph nodes, edge rules, ingestion flow, and AI query patterns.

### Assistant
> Yep. This is a **real stack now**, not just an idea.
> 
> From the screenshot, you currently have four functional layers:
> 
> - **Graph layer:** `graphiti` + `neo4j`
> - **API/data layer:** `qiarchive-api` + `qiarchive-postgres`
> - **ingestion layer:** `paperless-ngx` + `redis` + another `Postgres`
> - **app layer:** `QiArchive`
> 
> That’s solid. But there’s one thing jumping out immediately:
> 
> ## You currently have **two Postgres instances**
> One under:
> - `qiarchive-postgres`
> 
> And another under:
> - `Ingestion > Postgres`
> 
> That is not automatically wrong, but it **can become stupid fast** if you do not define roles clearly.
> 
> ## What this likely means
> 
> ### `qiarchive-postgres`
> This should be your **main operational database**:
> - cases
> - actors
> - events
> - documents
> - filings
> - violations
> - damages
> - deadlines
> - app auth / metadata
> - AI retrieval metadata
> - structured views
> 
> ### `Ingestion > Postgres`
> This is probably for **Paperless-NGX internals only**:
> - paperless documents
> - tags
> - correspondents
> - OCR state
> - ingestion job metadata
> 
> If that second Postgres is just Paperless’s app database, fine. Leave it alone.
> 
> If you were planning to also store your case truth there too, no. Don’t do that. That’s how you end up with duplicated facts and drift.
> 
> ## Clean architecture reading of your screenshot
> 
> ### 1. `Paperless-NGX`
> This is your intake and OCR machine.
> Use it for:
> - scanned documents
> - PDFs
> - letters
> - exhibits
> - mail
> - notices
> - sheriff docs
> - court docs
> - bureau responses
> 
> Its job:
> - ingest
> - OCR
> - classify
> - tag
> - retain original files
> 
> Not its job:
> - be the case brain
> - hold your full litigation logic
> - replace structured tables
> 
> ### 2. `qiarchive-postgres`
> This should be the **case brain**.
> 
> This is where your actual normalized case model should live:
> - one document record
> - one event record
> - one actor record
> - linked violations
> - linked damages
> - linked filings
> 
> This is the source of truth.
> 
> ### 3. `Graphiti + Neo4j`
> This is the **relationship and memory layer**.
> 
> Perfect for:
> - entity extraction
> - contradiction discovery
> - relationship traversal
> - “show me everything connected to X”
> - long-form AI recall
> 
> Not perfect for:
> - authoritative deadline tracking
> - precise ledger math
> - canonical docket management
> 
> ### 4. `qiarchive-api`
> This is the thing that should unify it all.
> 
> It should:
> - query Postgres first
> - enrich with Graphiti/Neo4j second
> - optionally pull OCR/source doc metadata from Paperless
> - return a clean answer to the UI or AI layer
> 
> ### 5. `QiArchive`
> This should be your review surface:
> - timeline views
> - issue views
> - docket views
> - evidence views
> - hearing prep views
> - search and recall
> 
> ## What’s good here
> 
> This stack actually maps well to your use case:
> 
> - **Paperless** handles intake chaos
> - **Postgres** handles structured truth
> - **Neo4j/Graphiti** handles connected context
> - **API** handles orchestration
> - **App** handles human review
> 
> That is a legit architecture.
> 
> ## What could go wrong
> 
> Here are the real risks:
> 
> ### 1. Duplicate truth
> If a document exists in:
> - Paperless
> - Postgres
> - Neo4j
> - maybe flat files too
> 
> …and you don’t use a stable `document_id`, you will create retrieval garbage.
> 
> ### 2. Unclear authority
> If Graphiti says one thing and Postgres says another, which wins?
> 
> Answer should be:
> - **Postgres wins for structured case facts**
> - **original file wins for raw evidence**
> - **Graph only enriches**
> 
> ### 3. Ingestion without normalization
> If you just pipe every OCR’d blob into graph memory, the AI will look smart while being sloppy.
> 
> ### 4. No record lifecycle
> You need to distinguish:
> - raw ingested file
> - normalized document record
> - extracted entities
> - linked legal significance
> - exhibit-ready item
> 
> Otherwise everything becomes “a document” and that’s useless.
> 
> ## My direct recommendation
> 
> You should define **system roles right now** before you keep building.
> 
> ## Recommended role map
> 
> ### Paperless-NGX = `intake_repository`
> Purpose:
> - ingest file
> - OCR file
> - tag source
> - preserve original
> 
> ### qiarchive-postgres = `case_system_of_record`
> Purpose:
> - normalized litigation data
> - primary reporting
> - views
> - deadlines
> - damages
> - evidence linking
> 
> ### Neo4j/Graphiti = `context_graph`
> Purpose:
> - relationships
> - entity memory
> - cross-document context
> - semantic traversal
> 
> ### qiarchive-api = `orchestration_layer`
> Purpose:
> - fetch from DB
> - enrich from graph
> - expose unified endpoints
> 
> ### QiArchive app = `review_interface`
> Purpose:
> - human retrieval
> - case review
> - hearing prep
> - evidence audit
> 
> ## What you need next
> 
> Not more infrastructure.  
> You need **data contract discipline**.
> 
> Specifically:
> 
> ### Required IDs
> Use stable IDs for:
> - `CASE-...`
> - `ACT-...`
> - `DOC-...`
> - `EVT-...`
> - `FIL-...`
> - `VIO-...`
> - `DAM-...`
> 
> ### Required sync rules
> For each ingested document:
> 
> 1. file lands in Paperless
> 2. OCR text extracted
> 3. API creates or updates `documents` row in main Postgres
> 4. entities/relations sent to Graphiti
> 5. graph stores references using the same `DOC-*` id
> 6. all case views read from main Postgres, not directly from Neo4j
> 
> That’s the discipline that keeps this from turning into expensive chaos.
> 
> ## Your immediate next decision
> 
> You need to choose one of these two paths:
> 
> ### Path A — smart path
> Keep Paperless and Graphiti as supporting systems, and make `qiarchive-postgres` the main case DB.
>

> 4. Push extracted entities into Graphiti with same IDs

>    - violation matrix
> 6. Add AI retrieval on top of those, not before

### Assistant
> Good. That’s the correct separation.
> 
> So the architecture is:
> 
> - **Paperless Postgres** = internal app database for Paperless only
> - **QiArchive Postgres** = canonical document registry and stable ID authority
> - **Graphiti/Neo4j** = relationship/context layer
> - **Agent** = reads and writes against QiArchive IDs, not Paperless internals
> 
> That is exactly how it should be.
> 
> ## What this means strategically
> 
> Your **`qi_documents` record is the permanent identity layer**.
> 
> So if a document:
> - gets reprocessed
> - gets re-embedded
> - gets linked to more events
> - gets mirrored into graph
> - gets cited in a filing
> - gets turned into an exhibit
> 
> …the **QiArchive document ID stays the same**.
> 
> That is the right design because it gives you:
> 
> - immutable reference points
> - cleaner agent memory
> - no dependency on Paperless’s internal IDs
> - safer joins across systems
> - better auditability
> 
> ## The rule you want
> 
> **Paperless is the intake source. QiArchive is the identity source.**
> 
> That means:
> - Paperless can ingest, OCR, classify, tag
> - QiArchive assigns the permanent `DOC-*` or `QI-DOC-*` ID
> - Every downstream system references the QiArchive ID
> - Never use Paperless IDs as the canonical external reference
> 
> ## Recommended model
> 
> ### In `qi_documents`
> Each row should have both:
> 
> - **canonical ID**: your permanent document ID
> - **source mapping fields**: references back to Paperless, file path, hash, etc.
> 
> Example fields:
> - `id` or `qi_document_id`
> - `canonical_doc_id`
> - `title`
> - `doc_type`
> - `source_system` = `paperless`
> - `source_document_id` = Paperless doc ID
> - `paperless_document_id`
> - `sha256`
> - `original_filename`
> - `mime_type`
> - `created_at`
> - `ocr_text`
> - `summary`
> - `status`
> 
> That gives you stable identity plus source traceability.
> 
> ## The important sync rule
> 
> When a file comes into Paperless:
> 
> 1. Paperless ingests file
> 2. OCR/extraction happens
> 3. QiArchive creates or updates the document row
> 4. QiArchive assigns permanent document ID
> 5. Agent references **QiArchive ID only**
> 6. Graph nodes also store **QiArchive ID only**
> 7. Paperless ID remains just a source pointer
> 
> That keeps the whole stack sane.
> 
> ## This also answers the split-table issue
> 
> If QiArchive is the stable ID authority, then your `qi_` tables are almost certainly the ones that matter.
> 
> So yes, your instinct is right:
> - **QiArchive schema is canonical**
> - Paperless schema is operational/internal
> - agent memory should anchor to QiArchive IDs
> 
> ## What I would lock in now
> 
> You need three non-negotiable identity rules:
> 
> ### 1. Immutable canonical IDs
> Once assigned, never reused, never changed.
> 
> ### 2. One document, one canonical row
> Even if re-uploaded or reprocessed, it resolves back to the same logical document when appropriate.
> 
> ### 3. All relationships point to canonical ID
> Not filename, not Paperless ID, not graph node ID.
> 
> ## The next real design question
> 
> Now that you’ve clarified identity ownership, the next thing is not infra.  
> It’s **object model**.
> 
> Because if docs have stable IDs, now you need the same pattern for:
> - events
> - actors
> - filings
> - violations
> - damages
> 
> That’s how the agent gets full case context instead of just document recall.
> 
> ## Clean system doctrine
> 
> Use this as the rule set:
> 
> - **Paperless** = document intake and OCR
> - **QiArchive** = canonical records and immutable IDs
> - **Graphiti/Neo4j** = relational memory indexed by canonical IDs
> - **Agent** = reasoner over canonical records, not app internals
> 
> That’s tight. That’s clean. That’s defensible.
> 
> ## Best next move
> 
> You should now define the **canonical ID schema and object registry** for all case objects, not just docs:
> 
> - `DOC-*`
> - `EVT-*`
> - `ACT-*`
> - `FIL-*`
> - `VIO-*`
> - `DAM-*`
> - `CASE-*`
> 
> Then everything can point to everything else without identity drift.
> 
> I can draft that as a **QiArchive canonical object model** next: IDs, tables, sync rules, and relationship map.

### User
> im using railway to host this what templates can we use to get up and running yesterday

### Assistant
> Fastest path: **don’t build a custom app first**. Stand up a usable case system in layers.
> 
> ## Best Railway template combo for “running yesterday”
> 
> ### Option 1 — fastest practical stack
> Use:
> - **NocoDB** for immediate tables, views, forms, filters, and API on top of Postgres. Railway has a one-click NocoDB template with Postgres and Redis, and there’s also a newer variant with persistent object storage. citeturn794105search0turn794105search1turn794105search3
> - **n8n** for ingestion, syncing, parsing, and automation. Railway has multiple n8n templates, including a simple PostgreSQL version and more production-ready variants. citeturn694687search0turn694687search1turn694687search3turn694687search4
> - **Neo4j** for graph context, which Railway supports with ready templates. citeturn694687search5turn694687search8
> - **your existing Graphiti service** for memory/relationship extraction if you keep it.
> - **Paperless-NGX** only for intake/OCR, since you already have it running.
> 
> That gets you:
> - database
> - grid/table views
> - linked records
> - forms for intake
> - automation
> - graph
> - OCR intake
> 
> Without waiting on a custom frontend.
> 
> ## Why I’d pick NocoDB first
> Because right now you need:
> - tables
> - views
> - relation fields
> - quick filtering
> - easy record editing
> - API access
> 
> NocoDB gives you that immediately on Railway with very little ceremony. citeturn794105search0turn794105search1turn794105search3
> 
> For your case, that means you can start with:
> - `cases`
> - `documents`
> - `events`
> - `actors`
> - `filings`
> - `violations`
> - `damages`
> - `deadlines`
> 
> And actually use them the same day.
> 
> ## Option 2 — better if you want a more polished long-term admin
> Use **Directus** instead of NocoDB.
> 
> Railway has verified Directus templates, including one with Redis and S3-compatible bucket storage, and a CMS-oriented starter. citeturn794105search4turn794105search6turn794105search7
> 
> Pick Directus if you want:
> - stronger permissions
> - cleaner admin UX
> - better content modeling
> - more polished APIs
> - something that can mature into a real internal app backend
> 
> But it’s a little more setup-thinky than NocoDB.
> 
> ## Option 3 — if you want dashboard/app-builder style now
> Use **Appsmith**.
> 
> Railway has an Appsmith template for internal tools and dashboards. citeturn794105search5
> 
> This is better if you specifically want:
> - custom dashboards
> - panels
> - workflow screens
> - a more “app-like” feel
> 
> But for raw data entry and building the schema fast, I’d still put **NocoDB or Directus underneath it**.
> 
> ## My blunt recommendation
> For **this case-only isolated stack**, do this:
> 
> ### Deploy order
> 1. **Fresh dedicated Postgres** for the case
> 2. **NocoDB** on top of that Postgres
> 3. **n8n** for ingestion flows
> 4. **Neo4j** if not already isolated for this case
> 5. **Graphiti** if you want graph memory now
> 6. Keep **Paperless** as intake only
> 
> ## The minimum useful stack
> If you want the absolute shortest path:
> 
> - **Postgres**
> - **NocoDB**
> - **n8n**
> 
> That alone is enough to start entering and reviewing:
> - documents
> - events
> - filings
> - violations
> - damages
> 
> Then bolt on graph second.
> 
> ## The best stack for your case specifically
> I’d do:
> 
> - **Postgres** = truth
> - **NocoDB** = immediate views/admin
> - **n8n** = ingestion/automation
> - **Paperless** = OCR intake
> - **Neo4j + Graphiti** = relationship layer
> 
> That is the best speed-to-usefulness ratio.
> 
> ## What I would not do
> Do **not** waste time right now on:
> - Next.js starter templates
> - Prisma starter templates as your first interface
> - custom React UI before schema is stable
> 
> Railway does have a Prisma Postgres starter, but that’s better once you already know your model and want to code the real app. It is not the fastest way to get case operations live this week. citeturn694687search6
> 
> ## Decision narrowing
> 
> ### Choose NocoDB if:
> you want spreadsheet-like speed and minimal friction.
> 
> ### Choose Directus if:
> you want stronger long-term structure and cleaner governance.
> 
> ### Choose Appsmith if:
> you already know the workflows and want to assemble dashboards fast.
> 
> For **you, today**, I’d pick:
> 
> **NocoDB + n8n + your dedicated Postgres**
> and keep the graph as a second layer.
> 
> That gets you operational fastest.
> 
> Next move should be either:
> **A)** I map the exact Railway deployment stack and service list, or  
> **B)** I draft the first 8 tables so you can build them in NocoDB without guessing.

### Assistant
> Pick **NocoDB Starter Pack**.
> 
> That’s the one most likely to get you moving fastest **without painting yourself into a dumb corner**.
> 
> ## Ranking for your use case
> 
> ### 1. **NocoDB Starter Pack** — best default
> Use this if it includes:
> - Postgres or proper DB
> - NocoDB
> - object storage or sane defaults
> - maybe Redis / supporting services
> 
> Why it wins:
> - fastest “usable yesterday” setup
> - fewer missing pieces
> - less manual wiring
> - better chance it’s already opinionated enough to work
> 
> For your case stack, that matters more than purity.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 2. **NocoDB with Object Storage** — strong second
> This is good if the starter pack is bloated or weird.
> 
> Why:
> - you will likely want attachments, exports, possibly exhibit assets
> - object storage is cleaner than trying to stuff everything awkwardly into DB workflows
> - better future-proofing than a barebones setup
> 
> This is probably the best choice if the starter pack feels too generic or heavy.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 3. **NocoDB + MinIO** — acceptable, but more moving parts
> This is basically the “object storage” route with explicit self-hosted S3-style storage.
> 
> Good if:
> - you know you want local/private object storage behavior
> - you’re okay with one more service to babysit
> 
> Bad if:
> - you’re trying to reduce complexity right now
> 
> For this case, I’d only choose this over the object-storage template if the object-storage template is unclear and this one is more explicit.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 4. **NocoDB + SQLite** — no
> Don’t do this for your case system.
> 
> Why:
> - too limited for where this is going
> - weaker concurrency story
> - not what you want if you’re doing linked records, attachments, ingestion, agents, and possible scaling
> - you already think in systems, not toy apps
> 
> SQLite is fine for tiny single-user throwaway setups.  
> This is not that.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 5. **Plain “NocoDB Open Source Airtable Alternative”** — only if it’s just the app
> This sounds like the most stripped-down version.
> 
> Only choose it if:
> - you want to wire your own Postgres manually
> - you know exactly what’s included
> - the other templates are messy
> 
> Otherwise, it’s probably just more setup work.
> 
> ## My actual recommendation
> 
> Choose in this order:
> 
> 1. **NocoDB Starter Pack**
> 2. **NocoDB with Object Storage**
> 3. **NocoDB + MinIO**
> 4. **Plain NocoDB**
> 5. **NocoDB + SQLite**
> 
> ## What you’re optimizing for
> Right now you need:
> - tables
> - linked records
> - filters/views
> - file attachment support
> - quick schema edits
> - low friction
> 
> You are **not** optimizing for elegance yet.  
> You are optimizing for **operational traction**.
> 
> ## What I’d do in your shoes
> Use either:
> 
> **A. NocoDB Starter Pack** if you want the least thinking  
> or  
> **B. NocoDB with Object Storage** if you want a slightly cleaner, more intentional setup
> 
> ## One warning
> Do **not** let NocoDB become your final truth architecture forever just because it gets you started fast.
> 
> Use it as:
> - immediate admin
> - views
> - manual review
> - light API layer
> 
> Then later, if needed, you can harden around Postgres, graph, and a custom app.
> 
> But for now?  
> **Starter Pack** is probably the right move.
> 
> Send me which one you choose, and I’ll tell you exactly how to structure the first base:
> - table names
> - fields
> - linked relationships
> - which 3 views to build first.

### User
> the start pack has nocodb, postgres, redis.

### Assistant
> Yep. **Use the Starter Pack.**
> 
> That gives you the right core:
> 
> - **Postgres** = actual data store
> - **NocoDB** = fast admin, tables, views, linked records
> - **Redis** = useful for caching/queues/background-ish support if needed later
> 
> For your case stack, that is the best balance of:
> - speed
> - enough structure
> - not too much crap
> - room to grow
> 
> ## Why this is the right one
> 
> Because:
> 
> - **SQLite** is too flimsy for this
> - **MinIO/object storage** is nice but not the first blocker
> - **plain NocoDB** makes you wire more yourself
> - **Starter Pack** gets you operational faster with the fewest dumb decisions
> 
> ## What to do right after deploy
> 
> Do **not** start by importing everything.
> 
> First create the core tables manually and keep them tight.
> 
> ## First 8 tables
> 
> Build these first:
> 
> 1. **cases**
> 2. **documents**
> 3. **actors**
> 4. **events**
> 5. **filings**
> 6. **violations**
> 7. **damages**
> 8. **deadlines**
> 
> ## Minimal field set
> 
> ### 1. `cases`
> - `id`
> - `case_code`
> - `case_name`
> - `cause_number`
> - `court`
> - `status`
> - `summary`
> 
> You’ll probably only have one case row right now.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 2. `documents`
> - `id`
> - `doc_id`  
> - `case_id` → link to `cases`
> - `title`
> - `doc_type`
> - `source_system`
> - `source_ref`
> - `file_path`
> - `sha256`
> - `document_date`
> - `ocr_text`
> - `summary`
> - `privilege_level`
> - `is_exhibit_candidate`
> 
> This becomes your anchor table.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 3. `actors`
> - `id`
> - `actor_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `name`
> - `actor_type`
> - `role_in_case`
> - `organization`
> - `email`
> - `phone`
> - `notes`
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 4. `events`
> - `id`
> - `event_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `event_date`
> - `event_type`
> - `title`
> - `summary`
> - `facts`
> - `legal_observations`
> - `ada_notes`
> - `impact`
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 5. `filings`
> - `id`
> - `filing_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `document_id` → link to `documents`
> - `filing_date`
> - `title`
> - `filing_type`
> - `filed_by_actor_id` → link to `actors`
> - `docket_text`
> - `outcome`
> - `notes`
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 6. `violations`
> - `id`
> - `violation_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `title`
> - `category`
> - `statute_or_rule`
> - `summary`
> - `status`
> - `severity`
> - `notes`
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 7. `damages`
> - `id`
> - `damage_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `damage_type`
> - `amount`
> - `estimated_amount`
> - `date_incurred`
> - `description`
> - `status`
> - `confidence_level`
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 8. `deadlines`
> - `id`
> - `deadline_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `title`
> - `due_date`
> - `deadline_type`
> - `status`
> - `source`
> - `notes`
> 
> ## Then add link tables
> 
> NocoDB can do linked records, but conceptually you need these relationships:
> 
> - documents ↔ events
> - documents ↔ actors
> - documents ↔ violations
> - events ↔ actors
> - events ↔ violations
> - violations ↔ damages
> - filings ↔ events
> 
> If NocoDB handles some with relation fields, fine.  
> But do **not** skip the relationship design in your head.
> 
> ## First 3 views to build
> 
> Build these immediately:
> 
> ### 1. **Master Timeline**
> Base: `events`  
> Show:
> - event date
> - title
> - type
> - linked docs
> - linked violations
> 
> ### 2. **Document Review**
> Base: `documents`  
> Show:
> - doc ID
> - title
> - type
> - date
> - source
> - exhibit candidate
> - linked event
> - linked violation
> 
> ### 3. **Violation Matrix**
> Base: `violations`  
> Show:
> - violation ID
> - title
> - statute/rule
> - status
> - linked events
> - linked documents
> - linked damages
> 
> That alone will already make the case more navigable.
> 
> ## Naming rule
> 
> Since this is a sealed case-specific stack, keep IDs case-scoped:
> 
> - `FC-DOC-0001`
> - `FC-ACT-0001`
> - `FC-EVT-0001`
> - `FC-FIL-0001`
> - `FC-VIO-0001`
> - `FC-DAM-0001`
> - `FC-DL-0001`
> 
> Clean, readable, stable.
> 
> ## What not to do in NocoDB
> 
> Do not:
> - dump giant raw notes into every field
> - make 40 tables on day one
> - import every folder blindly
> - rely on attachments alone without metadata
> - turn one record into a whole essay blob
> 
> Keep records atomic.
> 
> ## Best rollout order
> 
> 1. deploy Starter Pack
> 2. create one `cases` row
> 3. create the 8 tables
> 4. define relation fields
> 5. enter 10–20 high-value records manually first
> 6. confirm the structure feels right
> 7. only then automate ingestion
> 
> That last part matters.  
> If you automate bad structure, you just create faster garbage.
> 
> ## Straight answer
> 
> **Yes, the Starter Pack is the right template.**  
> It gives you the cleanest fast start for a private, isolated case stack on Railway.
> 
> Next step is to map the exact NocoDB table definitions in copy-paste form so you can build them without guessing.

### User
> we are not using the work violations we are saying issues instead.

### Assistant
> Good. **Use `issues`, not `violations`.**
> 
> That’s the better operational label right now because:
> 
> - it’s broader
> - it avoids prematurely locking legal conclusions
> - it lets you track weak, emerging, disputed, and confirmed problems in one place
> - it’s cleaner for internal review and safer before something is fully proven
> 
> “Violation” sounds adjudicated.  
> “Issue” lets you track:
> - suspected defect
> - confirmed defect
> - dispute point
> - legal theory
> - proof gap
> - risk item
> 
> That is the smarter case-ops word.
> 
> ## Rename the table
> 
> Change:
> 
> - `violations` → `issues`
> 
> And use fields like:
> 
> ### `issues`
> - `id`
> - `issue_id`
> - `case_id`
> - `title`
> - `issue_type`
> - `category`
> - `statute_or_rule`
> - `summary`
> - `status`
> - `severity`
> - `proof_status`
> - `notes`
> 
> ## Better status model for `issues`
> 
> Instead of acting like everything is already a proven violation, use statuses like:
> 
> - `identified`
> - `under_review`
> - `supported`
> - `disputed`
> - `high_risk`
> - `escalated`
> - `preserved_for_claim`
> - `deprioritized`
> 
> That gives you room to think clearly.
> 
> ## Better relationship map
> 
> Update the link logic to:
> 
> - documents ↔ issues
> - events ↔ issues
> - issues ↔ damages
> - filings ↔ issues
> - actors ↔ issues
> 
> ## Revised first 8 tables
> 
> Use:
> 
> 1. `cases`
> 2. `documents`
> 3. `actors`
> 4. `events`
> 5. `filings`
> 6. `issues`
> 7. `damages`
> 8. `deadlines`
> 
> ## Suggested `issue_type` values
> 
> Keep these controlled:
> 
> - `service`
> - `notice`
> - `jurisdiction`
> - `ada`
> - `due_process`
> - `credit_reporting`
> - `accounting`
> - `post_judgment_conduct`
> - `property_handling`
> - `damages_support`
> - `regulatory`
> - `procedural`
> - `evidence`
> - `settlement`
> 
> ## Suggested `proof_status` values
> 
> Use something like:
> 
> - `unverified`
> - `partially_supported`
> - `document_supported`
> - `multi_source_supported`
> - `contested`
> - `needs_exhibit`
> - `needs_witness`
> - `needs_calculation`
> 
> That’s much more useful than pretending every problem is already fully matured.
> 
> ## Naming rule update
> 
> IDs become:
> 
> - `FC-ISS-0001`
> 
> instead of:
> - `FC-VIO-0001`
> 
> ## Bottom line
> 
> Yes — **issues is the right word**.
> 
> It keeps the system sharper, safer, and more flexible.
> 
> Your corrected table set is now:
> 
> - `cases`
> - `documents`
> - `actors`
> - `events`
> - `filings`
> - `issues`
> - `damages`
> - `deadlines`
> 
> Next step should be building the **exact NocoDB field list using `issues` terminology** so you can enter this cleanly the first time.

### User
> i can import csv files

### Assistant
> Perfect. Then **don’t hand-build these tables row by row** like a peasant. Use CSV import as the bootstrap path.
> 
> ## Best move
> 
> Start with **7 CSVs**, imported in this order:
> 
> 1. `cases.csv`
> 2. `actors.csv`
> 3. `documents.csv`
> 4. `events.csv`
> 5. `issues.csv`
> 6. `filings.csv`
> 7. `damages.csv`
> 8. `deadlines.csv`
> 
> Then do **link tables** after the core records exist.
> 
> ## Important rule
> 
> For NocoDB imports to not get stupid, every CSV needs a **stable text ID column** you control.
> 
> Use these:
> - `case_id`
> - `actor_id`
> - `doc_id`
> - `event_id`
> - `issue_id`
> - `filing_id`
> - `damage_id`
> - `deadline_id`
> 
> Do **not** rely on auto-number IDs for cross-table references in your source files.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Core CSV headers
> 
> ## 1) `cases.csv`
> 
> ```csv
> case_id,case_code,case_name,cause_number,court,status,summary

> ```

>

> FC-ACT-0001,FC-CASE-0001,Cody Rice-Velasquez,person,defendant,,,,
> FC-ACT-0002,FC-CASE-0001,Financial Center First Credit Union,organization,plaintiff,Financial Center First Credit Union,,,
> FC-ACT-0003,FC-CASE-0001,Marion Superior Court 3,government,court,Marion Superior Court 3,,,
> ```

> - `person`
> - `organization`
> - `government`

> - `agency`
> - `vendor`

> FC-DOC-0002,FC-CASE-0001,Credit Report,credit_report,manual_import,,,2026-03-00,,internal,true

> # Link-table CSVs
> 
> After those import clean, do these.
> 
> ## `document_event_links.csv`

> ```

> ```

> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # Import order that won’t screw you
> 
> Import in this order:
> 
> 1. `cases.csv`
> 2. `actors.csv`
> 3. `documents.csv`
> 4. `events.csv`
> 5. `issues.csv`
> 6. `filings.csv`
> 7. `damages.csv`
> 8. `deadlines.csv`
> 9. all link tables
> 
> If NocoDB asks you to map columns, map by the text IDs first.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What to do right now
> 
> Do **not** try to import every legacy file immediately.
> 
> Bootstrap with:
> - 1 case
> - 10 actors
> - 20 documents
> - 15 events
> - 10 issues
> - 10 filings
> - 8 damages
> - 5 deadlines
> 
> That’s enough to test whether the structure actually works.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation
> 
> Your next move should be:
> **build the CSV templates once, clean, and then map your current markdown/folder data into them.**
> 
> I can do the next piece as either:
> - a **copy-paste CSV starter pack** with blank rows ready to fill, or
> - a **field mapping plan** from your current case files into these CSVs.

### User
> we can do json files too

### Assistant
> Yes. And honestly, **JSON is better than CSV** once you start dealing with nested relationships, multi-value fields, and AI ingestion.
> 
> Use them **for different jobs**:
> 
> ## Use CSV for
> - quick bulk import into NocoDB
> - simple tables
> - one-record-per-row data
> - fast cleanup in spreadsheets
> 
> ## Use JSON for
> - richer source-of-truth exports
> - nested linked data
> - ingestion pipelines
> - graph feeding
> - agent context packets
> - preserving structure without flattening everything into ugly columns
> 
> So the smart play is:
> 
> - **CSV = import format**
> - **JSON = canonical interchange / ingestion format**
> 
> ## My recommendation
> 
> For this case stack, keep a `/seed` or `/bootstrap` folder with both:
> 
> - `cases.csv`
> - `actors.csv`
> - `documents.csv`
> - etc.
> 
> and also:
> 
> - `cases.json`
> - `actors.json`
> - `documents.json`
> - `events.json`
> - `issues.json`
> 
> That way:
> - NocoDB gets fed quickly
> - your agent pipeline has cleaner structured data
> - later migrations don’t depend on spreadsheet logic
> 
> ## Better long-term shape
> 
> ### Flat table JSON
> Good for direct table seeding.
> 
> Example `issues.json`:
> 
> ```json
> [
>   {
>     "issue_id": "FC-ISS-0001",
>     "case_id": "FC-CASE-0001",
>     "title": "Service and notice defect",
>     "issue_type": "service",
>     "category": "jurisdiction",
>     "statute_or_rule": "TR 4.1 / TR 4.15",
>     "summary": "",
>     "status": "identified",
>     "severity": "high",
>     "proof_status": "partially_supported",
>     "notes": ""
>   },
>   {
>     "issue_id": "FC-ISS-0002",
>     "case_id": "FC-CASE-0001",
>     "title": "ADA accommodation and notice concerns",
>     "issue_type": "ada",
>     "category": "access",
>     "statute_or_rule": "",
>     "summary": "",
>     "status": "identified",
>     "severity": "high",
>     "proof_status": "partially_supported",
>     "notes": ""
>   }
> ]
> ```
> 
> That’s clean for seed scripts and API imports.
> 
> ## Even better: one case bundle JSON
> 
> For AI and graph ingestion, you may also want a **single case bundle file**.
> 
> Example:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "case": {
>     "case_id": "FC-CASE-0001",
>     "case_code": "FCFCU_CASE",
>     "case_name": "Financial Center First Credit Union v. Cody Rice-Velasquez",
>     "cause_number": "49D03-2501-MF-002559",
>     "court": "Marion Superior Court 3",
>     "status": "active"
>   },
>   "actors": [],
>   "documents": [],
>   "events": [],
>   "issues": [],
>   "filings": [],
>   "damages": [],
>   "deadlines": [],
>   "links": {
>     "document_event_links": [],
>     "document_issue_links": [],
>     "event_issue_links": [],
>     "issue_damage_links": [],
>     "filing_issue_links": [],
>     "event_actor_links": []
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> That’s much better for:
> - agent loading
> - graph sync
> - backups
> - portability
> - versioning
> 
> ## Straight answer on what you should do
> 
> Use **JSON as the master bootstrap format**, then generate CSV from it when needed.
> 
> Why:
> - JSON handles arrays and nested data better
> - easier to validate
> - easier to diff in Git
> - easier to feed into scripts
> - easier to transform into graph nodes/edges later
> 
> CSV is still useful, but it’s the **dumber transport layer**.
> 
> ## Best file strategy
> 
> I’d structure it like this:
> 
> ```text
> /bootstrap
>   /json
>     cases.json
>     actors.json
>     documents.json
>     events.json
>     issues.json
>     filings.json
>     damages.json
>     deadlines.json
>     links.json
>     case_bundle.json
>   /csv
>     cases.csv
>     actors.csv
>     documents.csv
>     events.csv
>     issues.csv
>     filings.csv
>     damages.csv
>     deadlines.csv
>     document_event_links.csv
>     document_issue_links.csv
>     event_issue_links.csv
>     issue_damage_links.csv
>     filing_issue_links.csv
>     event_actor_links.csv
> ```
> 
> That gives you both machine-clean and import-friendly versions.
> 
> ## Bottom line
> 
> Yes, JSON is absolutely valid here.
> 
> And really:
> - **JSON should be your structured source**
> - **CSV should be your NocoDB convenience layer**
> 
> That’s the clean way to do it.
> 
> I can draft the **exact JSON schemas and starter files** for your 8 core objects next.

### User
> yes please.

### Assistant
> Perfect. Here’s the **clean JSON starter pack** for the isolated FCFCU case stack.

> Use this as your `/bootstrap/json/` seed set.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Folder layout
> 
> ```text
> /bootstrap/json/
>   00_case.json
>   01_actors.json
>   02_documents.json
>   03_events.json
>   04_issues.json
>   05_filings.json
>   06_damages.json
>   07_deadlines.json
>   08_links.json
>   99_case_bundle.json
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # 1) `00_case.json`
> 
> This is the top-level case record.
> 
> ```json
> [
>   {
>     "case_id": "FC-CASE-0001",
>     "case_code": "FCFCU_CASE",
>     "case_name": "Financial Center First Credit Union v. Cody Rice-Velasquez",
>     "cause_number": "49D03-2501-MF-002559",
>     "court": "Marion Superior Court 3",
>     "jurisdiction": "Marion County, Indiana",
>     "status": "active",
>     "case_type": "mortgage_foreclosure",
>     "sensitivity_level": "restricted",
>     "summary": "",
>     "notes": ""
>   }
> ]
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # 2) `01_actors.json`
> 
> Use this for people, orgs, court, agencies, counsel, sheriff, etc.
> 
> ```json
> [
>   {
>     "actor_id": "FC-ACT-0001",
>     "case_id": "FC-CASE-0001",
>     "name": "Cody Rice-Velasquez",
>     "actor_type": "person",
>     "role_in_case": "defendant",
>     "organization": "",
>     "email": "",
>     "phone": "",
>     "address": "",
>     "status": "active",
>     "notes": ""
>   },
>   {
>     "actor_id": "FC-ACT-0002",
>     "case_id": "FC-CASE-0001",
>     "name": "Financial Center First Credit Union",
>     "actor_type": "organization",
>     "role_in_case": "plaintiff",
>     "organization": "Financial Center First Credit Union",
>     "email": "",
>     "phone": "",
>     "address": "",
>     "status": "active",
>     "notes": ""
>   },
>   {

>     "name": "Marion Superior Court 3",

>     "phone": "",
>     "address": "",
>     "status": "active",
>     "notes": ""
>   }
> ]
> ```
>

> ```json
> {

>     "person",
>     "organization",
>     "government",

>     "agency",
>     "vendor",

> # 10) `99_case_bundle.json`

>

> 
> # Recommended field rules
> 
> Use these rules from day one:
> 
> ## IDs
> - immutable
> - uppercase
> - zero-padded
> - never reused
> - never changed after assignment
> 
> Examples:
> - `FC-CASE-0001`
> - `FC-DOC-0001`
> - `FC-EVT-0001`
> - `FC-ISS-0001`
> 
> ## Dates
> Use ISO only:
> 
> ```json
> "2026-03-13"
> ```
> 
> No:
> - `3/13/26`
> - `March 13, 2026`
> - `yesterday`
> 
> ## Empty values
> Use:
> - `""` for blank text
> - `null` for unknown numeric/date-like values where appropriate
> - `false` / `true` for booleans
> 
> ## Text discipline
> Do not put giant essays in every field.
> Use:
> - `summary` = short useful abstract
> - `facts` = facts only
> - `notes` = cleanup/misc
> - `legal_observations` = legal significance, not emotion dump
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best practical use
> 
> ## JSON as source of truth
> Use these files for:
> - seeding
> - versioning
> - script ingestion
> - graph sync
> - agent context
> 
> ## CSV as import convenience
> Generate CSV from JSON when NocoDB wants flatter imports.
> 
> That means your smart workflow is:
> 
> 1. maintain JSON cleanly
> 2. transform to CSV when needed
> 3. import into NocoDB
> 4. sync graph from JSON or DB
> 
> ---
> 
> # What I’d do next
> 
> Your next move should be:
> 
> 1. create these JSON files
> 2. put in only the first 10–20 high-value records
> 3. test the model before mass import
> 4. then generate CSVs from the same source
> 
> That avoids industrializing bad structure.
> 
> If you want, next I’ll give you the **matching blank templates** with empty arrays and placeholder objects only, so you can start filling them without stripping out my example rows.

### Assistant
> Yes. That is the move.
> 
> Stop iterating the schema in the UI.  
> **Use the codebase export as the source, transform once, import once, then clean inside the app only after the data is actually in there.**
> 
> Right now your problem is not ideas. It’s that you’ve been doing too much hand-designed setup before proving the ingestion path.
> 
> ## What to do instead
> 
> Build a **one-time migration pipeline**:
> 
> ### Phase 1
> Export everything you already have from the codebase into a **normalized intermediate format**.
> 
> Not straight into NocoDB first.  
> Not straight into graph first.
> 
> Use one intermediate directory like:
> 
> ```text
> /export_case/
>   raw_export.json
>   normalized/
>     00_case.json
>     01_actors.json
>     02_documents.json
>     03_events.json
>     04_issues.json
>     05_filings.json
>     06_damages.json
>     07_deadlines.json
>     08_links.json
> ```
> 
> That becomes the one-time import pack.
> 
> ## Why this is the right play
> 
> Because your real source material is already spread across:
> - markdown
> - folder names
> - structured content files
> - PDFs
> - logs
> - notes
> - maybe frontmatter
> - maybe code/constants/config
> 
> The codebase exporter can already see all that.  
> So instead of rebuilding by hand, you let the exporter extract what exists and shape it into importable objects.
> 
> ## The real strategy
> 
> You need **three layers**, not fifty.
> 
> ### 1. Raw export
> Dump everything as-is from the repo.
> 
> This is your evidence-preserving snapshot.
> 
> Example:
> - file path
> - filename
> - raw text
> - frontmatter
> - hashes
> - modified time
> - section headings
> 
> ### 2. Normalizer
> A script maps raw exported content into your case objects:
> - case
> - actor
> - document
> - event
> - issue
> - filing
> - damage
> - deadline
> - links
> 
> ### 3. Importer
> Take those normalized JSON/CSV files and import them into NocoDB/Postgres once.
> 
> That’s it.
> 
> ## Don’t aim for perfect on pass one
> 
> Aim for:
> - **80% imported correctly**
> - **stable IDs**
> - **no duplicate chaos**
> - **manual cleanup in app after import**
> 
> That is good enough.
> 
> Trying to perfectly infer every relationship before import is why you keep getting stuck.
> 
> ## Minimal one-time migration design
> 
> ### Raw export record shape
> Each file from your codebase export should become something like:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "source_path": "facts/events/2025-12-09_removal.md",
>   "filename": "2025-12-09_removal.md",
>   "file_type": "markdown",
>   "sha256": "...",
>   "frontmatter": {},
>   "content": "...full text...",
>   "headings": ["Summary", "Facts", "Questions / Flags"],
>   "last_modified": "2026-03-13T12:00:00Z"
> }
> ```
> 
> This is the preservation layer.
> 
> ### Normalized output shape
> Then map into objects like:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "event_id": "FC-EVT-0042",
>   "case_id": "FC-CASE-0001",
>   "event_date": "2025-12-09",
>   "event_type": "removal",
>   "title": "Writ of assistance executed",
>   "summary": "...",
>   "facts": "...",
>   "legal_observations": "...",
>   "source_path": "facts/events/2025-12-09_removal.md"
> }
> ```
> 
> That is the import layer.
> 
> ## Your best path now
> 
> Use the exporter to create these buckets:
> 
> ### Direct buckets
> These are easiest to infer:
> - `documents`
> - `events`
> - `issues`
> - `damages`
> - `actors`
> 
> ### Derived buckets
> These can be second pass:
> - `filings`
> - `deadlines`
> - `links`
> 
> Because filings and links often require more interpretation.
> 
> ## Decision narrowing
> 
> Here’s the simplest workable mapping logic:
> 
> ### If file lives under:
> - `facts/events` → candidate `event`
> - `facts/actors` → candidate `actor`
> - `facts/damages` → candidate `damage`
> - `facts/issues` or old `violations` → candidate `issue`
> - `pleadings` / `orders` / `complaints` / `service` / `evidence` → candidate `document`
> - docket/timeline summaries → candidate `filing` or `event`
> 
> ### Then assign:
> - one canonical object type
> - one stable ID
> - source path
> - title
> - parsed text sections
> - confidence flag if needed
> 
> ## The key rule
> 
> Every normalized object must carry:
> 
> - `source_path`
> - `source_hash`
> - `import_batch`
> - `case_id`
> 
> That way if something looks off later, you can trace it back without guessing.
> 
> ## What not to do
> 
> Do not:
> - manually rebuild data that already exists in files
> - try to fully automate graph relationships before base import
> - hand-enter hundreds of records in NocoDB
> - keep changing field names every day
> 
> That’s why you’re exhausted. The process is leaking energy.
> 
> ## The better operational sequence
> 
> 1. run full repo export
> 2. produce raw structured dump
> 3. write one transformer script
> 4. emit normalized JSON files
> 5. optionally emit CSV from those JSON files
> 6. import once into NocoDB
> 7. review and fix edge cases inside the app
> 8. only after that, feed graph
> 
> That is the clean path.
> 
> ## My blunt read
> 
> You do **not** need more templates right now.  
> You need a **conversion contract**.
> 
> The contract is:
> 
> **existing case repo → normalized import pack → one-time import**
> 
> That’s the thing that ends the loop.
> 
> ## What I need to help you fastest
> 
> Paste the current export shape from your script. Even a sample of:
> - one exported markdown file object
> - one PDF/file object
> - one folder summary object
> 
> Then I can map it directly into the normalized import format without more abstract nonsense.

> Please send over the blank templates and I'll get to work on the migration.#!/usr/bin/env python3
> """
> dump_tree_with_ocr.py
> 
> Walk from the script's directory, ignore dot-directories (and their contents),

> Special handling:
> - PDFs: copy to tmp, render pages to JPG, OCR each page, output text
> - Images: copy to tmp, convert to JPG, OCR, output text
> 
> Output:
> - single .txt file in script directory
> """
> 
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import os
> import sys
> import time
> import shutil
> import tempfile
> from pathlib import Path
> from typing import Iterable, Tuple
> 
> # ----------------------------
> # Config
> # ----------------------------
> OUTPUT_FILENAME = "repo_dump_tree_with_contents_and_ocr.txt"
> 
> # Skip dot-dirs automatically; add more directory names here
> EXTRA_SKIP_DIR_NAMES = {
>     "__pycache__",
>     "node_modules",
>     "dist",
>     "build",
>     ".obsidian",
>     ".venv",
>     ".infio_json_db",
> }
> 
> # Image/PDF handling
> OCR_IMAGE_EXTS = {".png", ".jpg", ".jpeg", ".webp", ".bmp", ".tif", ".tiff"}
> PDF_EXTS = {".pdf"}
> 
> # Text files that should always be included (CSV, MD, TXT, etc.)
> TEXT_FILE_EXTS = {".csv", ".md", ".txt", ".json", ".yaml", ".yml", ".log"}
> 
> # Skip some common binary/noisy file types (non-image/pdf)
> SKIP_FILE_EXTS = {
>     ".zip",
>     ".tar",
>     ".gz",
>     ".7z",
>     ".rar",
>     ".exe",
>     ".dll",
>     ".so",
>     ".dylib",
>     ".mp4",
>     ".mov",
>     ".mkv",
>     ".mp3",
>     ".wav",
>     ".woff",
>     ".woff2",
>     ".ttf",
>     ".otf",
>     ".ico",
> }
> 
> # Hard limits
> MAX_FILE_BYTES = 300_000  # for normal text files
> MAX_TEXT_CHARS_TO_WRITE = 300_000  # truncate big outputs
> MAX_PDF_PAGES_OCR = 50  # keep you from nuking your day
> 
> # Tree formatting
> INDENT = "  "
> 
> # If Tesseract isn't in PATH, set this (Windows example):
> # TESSERACT_CMD = r"C:\Program Files\Tesseract-OCR\tesseract.exe"
> TESSERACT_CMD = None
> 
> 
> def is_dot_dir(path: Path) -> bool:
>     return path.is_dir() and path.name.startswith(".")
> 
> 
> def should_skip_dir(path: Path) -> bool:
>     if is_dot_dir(path):
>         return True
>     if path.name in EXTRA_SKIP_DIR_NAMES:
>         return True
>     return False
> 
> 
> def iter_children_sorted(dir_path: Path) -> Iterable[Path]:
>     try:
>         kids = list(dir_path.iterdir())
>     except OSError:
>         return []
>     kids.sort(key=lambda p: (0 if p.is_dir() else 1, p.name.lower()))
>     return kids
> 
> 
> def safe_stat_size(path: Path) -> int | None:
>     try:
>         return path.stat().st_size
>     except OSError:
>         return None
> 
> 
> def should_skip_file(path: Path) -> Tuple[bool, str]:
>     ext = path.suffix.lower()
> 
>     # PDFs/images are handled separately, don’t skip them by ext
>     if ext in PDF_EXTS or ext in OCR_IMAGE_EXTS:
>         return False, ""
> 
>     # skip some known binaries
>     if ext in SKIP_FILE_EXTS:
>         return True, f"skipped (ext {ext})"
> 
>     size = safe_stat_size(path)
>     if size is None:
>         return True, "skipped (cannot stat)"
>     if size > MAX_FILE_BYTES:
>         return True, f"skipped (too large: {size} bytes)"
> 
>     return False, ""
> 
> 
> def read_text_file_safely(path: Path) -> Tuple[str, str]:
>     """
>     Returns (text, note). Note is non-empty if truncated or decoding issues.
>     """
>     try:
>         raw = path.read_bytes()
>     except OSError as e:
>         return "", f"ERROR reading file: {e}"
> 
>     if b"\x00" in raw:
>         return "", "skipped (binary content detected)"
> 
>     note = ""
>     try:
>         text = raw.decode("utf-8")
>     except UnicodeDecodeError:
>         text = raw.decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
>         note = "NOTE: decoded with replacement (non-utf8 bytes present)"
> 
>     if len(text) > MAX_TEXT_CHARS_TO_WRITE:
>         text = text[:MAX_TEXT_CHARS_TO_WRITE]
>         note = (
>             note + " | " if note else ""
>         ) + f"NOTE: truncated to {MAX_TEXT_CHARS_TO_WRITE} chars"
> 
>     return text, note
> 
> 
> def try_import_ocr() -> Tuple[object | None, object | None, str]:
>     """
>     Returns (pytesseract, PIL.Image, note)
>     """
>     try:
>         import pytesseract  # type: ignore
>     except Exception as e:
>         return None, None, f"OCR unavailable: failed to import pytesseract ({e})"
> 
>     try:
>         from PIL import Image  # type: ignore
>     except Exception as e:
>         return None, None, f"OCR unavailable: failed to import Pillow/PIL ({e})"
> 
>     if TESSERACT_CMD:
>         try:
>             pytesseract.pytesseract.tesseract_cmd = TESSERACT_CMD
>         except Exception:
>             pass
> 
>     # Smoke-test: if tesseract binary missing, pytesseract errors on call; we handle later.
>     return pytesseract, Image, ""
> 
> 
> def ocr_jpg(jpg_path: Path, pytesseract, Image) -> Tuple[str, str]:
>     """
>     OCR a JPG file; returns (text, note)
>     """
>     try:
>         img = Image.open(jpg_path)
>         text = pytesseract.image_to_string(img)
>         text = text.strip()
>         if len(text) > MAX_TEXT_CHARS_TO_WRITE:
>             text = text[:MAX_TEXT_CHARS_TO_WRITE]
>             return (
>                 text,
>                 f"NOTE: OCR output truncated to {MAX_TEXT_CHARS_TO_WRITE} chars",
>             )
>         return text, ""
>     except Exception as e:
>         return "", f"OCR error: {e}"
> 
> 
> def convert_image_to_jpg(src: Path, tmp_dir: Path, Image) -> Tuple[Path | None, str]:
>     """
>     Copy image to tmp and convert to JPG (even if already jpg).
>     """
>     try:
>         copied = tmp_dir / f"{src.stem}{src.suffix.lower()}"
>         shutil.copy2(src, copied)
> 
>         img = Image.open(copied)
>         rgb = img.convert("RGB")
>         out = tmp_dir / f"{src.stem}.jpg"
>         rgb.save(out, "JPEG", quality=90)
>         return out, ""
>     except Exception as e:
>         return None, f"image->jpg conversion error: {e}"
> 
> 
> def pdf_to_jpgs(src: Path, tmp_dir: Path) -> Tuple[list[Path], str]:
>     """
>     Copy PDF to tmp and render pages to JPG using PyMuPDF (fitz).
>     """
>     try:
>         import fitz  # PyMuPDF
>     except Exception as e:
>         return [], f"PDF render unavailable: failed to import PyMuPDF/fitz ({e})"
> 
>     try:
>         copied = tmp_dir / src.name
>         shutil.copy2(src, copied)
> 
>         doc = fitz.open(str(copied))
>         page_count = doc.page_count
>         if page_count > MAX_PDF_PAGES_OCR:
>             return (
>                 [],
>                 f"skipped OCR (pdf has {page_count} pages > MAX_PDF_PAGES_OCR={MAX_PDF_PAGES_OCR})",
>             )
> 
>         out_paths: list[Path] = []
>         for i in range(page_count):
>             page = doc.load_page(i)
>             # 2x scale for better OCR readability
>             mat = fitz.Matrix(2, 2)
>             pix = page.get_pixmap(matrix=mat, alpha=False)
> 
>             out = tmp_dir / f"{src.stem}_page_{i + 1:03d}.jpg"
>             pix.save(str(out))
>             out_paths.append(out)
> 
>         doc.close()
>         return out_paths, ""
>     except Exception as e:
>         return [], f"PDF render error: {e}"
> 
> 
> def dump_file_with_content_or_ocr(
>     path: Path, rel: Path, tmp_dir: Path, out_lines: list[str], depth: int
> ) -> None:
>     prefix = INDENT * depth
>     ext = path.suffix.lower()
> 
>     pytesseract, Image, ocr_note = try_import_ocr()
> 
>     # --- PDF OCR ---
>     if ext in PDF_EXTS:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}📄 {rel}")
>         if not pytesseract or not Image:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {ocr_note} ---")
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- skipped OCR for PDF ---")
>             return
> 
>         jpgs, note = pdf_to_jpgs(path, tmp_dir)
>         if note:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {note} ---")
>         if not jpgs:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}[no OCR output]")
>             return
> 
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- BEGIN OCR (PDF): {rel} ---")

>             text, n2 = ocr_jpg(jpg, pytesseract, Image)
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}[PAGE {j}]")
>             if n2:
>                 out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {n2} ---")
>             out_lines.append(
>                 f"{prefix}{INDENT}{text if text else '[no text detected]'}"
>             )
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}")  # blank line between pages
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- END OCR (PDF): {rel} ---")
>         return
> 
>     # --- Image OCR ---
>     if ext in OCR_IMAGE_EXTS:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}📄 {rel}")
>         if not pytesseract or not Image:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {ocr_note} ---")
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- skipped OCR for image ---")
>             return
> 
>         jpg, note = convert_image_to_jpg(path, tmp_dir, Image)
>         if note:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {note} ---")
>         if not jpg:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}[no OCR output]")
>             return
> 
>         text, n2 = ocr_jpg(jpg, pytesseract, Image)
>         if n2:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {n2} ---")
> 
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- BEGIN OCR (IMAGE): {rel} ---")
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}{text if text else '[no text detected]'}")
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- END OCR (IMAGE): {rel} ---")
>         return
> 
>     # --- CSV and other text files ---
>     # Check if this is a known text file type that should always be included
>     is_text_file = ext in TEXT_FILE_EXTS
>     
>     skip, why = should_skip_file(path)
>     
>     # Special handling for CSV files - add indicator
>     if ext == ".csv":
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}� {rel}  [CSV DATA]")
>     else:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}�📄 {rel}")
>     
>     if skip and not is_text_file:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {why} ---")
>         return
> 
>     text, note = read_text_file_safely(path)
>     if note:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- {note} ---")
> 
>     # Add special header for CSV files
>     if ext == ".csv":
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- BEGIN CSV DATA: {rel} ---")
>     else:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- BEGIN FILE: {rel} ---")
>     
>     if text:
>         for line in text.splitlines():

>     else:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}[no text output]")
>     
>     # Add special footer for CSV files
>     if ext == ".csv":
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- END CSV DATA: {rel} ---")
>     else:
>         out_lines.append(f"{prefix}{INDENT}--- END FILE: {rel} ---")
> 
> 
> def dump_dir(
>     root: Path, dir_path: Path, depth: int, tmp_dir: Path, out_lines: list[str]
> ) -> None:
>     for child in iter_children_sorted(dir_path):
>         rel = child.relative_to(root)
>         prefix = INDENT * depth
> 
>         if child.is_dir():
>             if should_skip_dir(child):
>                 out_lines.append(f"{prefix}📁 {rel}/  [skipped dir]")
>                 continue
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}📁 {rel}/")
>             dump_dir(root, child, depth + 1, tmp_dir, out_lines)
> 
>         elif child.is_file():
>             dump_file_with_content_or_ocr(child, rel, tmp_dir, out_lines, depth)
> 
>         else:
>             out_lines.append(f"{prefix}❓ {rel}  [skipped non-regular file]")
> 
> 
> def main() -> int:
>     script_path = Path(__file__).resolve()
>     root = script_path.parent
> 
>     # temp folder inside project root (NOT dot-prefixed, so you can inspect it if needed)
>     tmp_dir = root / "_tmp_ocr"
>     tmp_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
> 
>     header = [
>         "DIRECTORY TREE + FILE CONTENTS + OCR (PDF/IMAGES)",
>         f"Generated: {time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')}",
>         f"Root: {root}",
>         f"Temp OCR folder: {tmp_dir}",
>         "",
>         "Legend: 📁 dir | 📄 file",
>         "",
>     ]
> 
>     out_lines: list[str] = []
>     out_lines.extend(header)
>     out_lines.append("📁 ./  (script root)")
>     dump_dir(root, root, 1, tmp_dir, out_lines)
> 
>     output_path = root / OUTPUT_FILENAME
>     try:
>         output_path.write_text("\n".join(out_lines) + "\n", encoding="utf-8")
>     except OSError as e:
>         print(f"Failed to write output file: {output_path}\n{e}", file=sys.stderr)
>         return 2
> 
>     print(f"Wrote: {output_path}")
>     return 0
> 
> 
> if __name__ == "__main__":
>     raise SystemExit(main())

> ---
> 
> ## The only rules that matter right now
> 
> Use these and stop changing them midstream:
> 
> - IDs are immutable
> - dates are ISO only: `YYYY-MM-DD`
> - blank text = `""`
> - unknown number = `null`
> - every migrated record gets:
>   - `source_path`
>   - `source_hash`
>   - `import_batch`
> 
> That gives you traceability and lets you fix bad imports later without guessing.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Based on your script
> Your dump script is actually enough to get a **raw preservation export**.  
> What you need next is a **transformer** that reads the dump or, better, reads the underlying files directly and emits these JSON objects.
> 
> The key correction: do **not** try to import the giant tree dump itself as the final database input. Use it as raw reference, then map into these templates.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Best next move
> Take your exporter and make it emit:
> 
> - one raw file inventory
> - one normalized JSON pack matching the templates above
> 
> That’s the path out of the iteration hell.
> 
> Paste me one sample of your export output after you run it, and I’ll help you map it into the final normalized shape with no more abstract nonsense.
