> # 1) Cleanup pass script spec
> 
> This script should do **report-first, move-second** behavior. Do **not** let it freestyle rename half your case into oblivion.
> 
> ## What it should do
> 
> ### Pass A — quarantine junk
> Move obvious garbage filenames from active areas into:
> 
> - `9_Archive/Quarantine/Junk_Filenames`
> 
> Rules:
> - filenames like `dfadadf.pdf`, `rttet.pdf`, `output.txt`, random mash files
> - temporary duplicate exports with no metadata value
> - files with names matching patterns like:
>   - `^[a-z]{3,}\.pdf$` only if nonsense
>   - repeated nonsense consonant/vowel garbage
>   - names like `copy`, `combinepdf`, `tmp`, `output`, `audit_report` only if they are outside approved utility/archive zones
> 
> But:
> - do **not** move anything from `9_Archive`
> - do **not** move anything from `2_Data`
> - do **not** move anything if filename matches known evidence patterns like `D####`, `EX-`, `C##`, date-prefixed evidence names
> 
> ### Pass B — naming normalization report
> Do **not rename yet** unless `--apply` is passed.
> 
> Generate a CSV of folder/file rename recommendations for:
> - lowercase folder drift:
>   - `damages` → `Damages`
>   - `complaint` → `Complaints`
>   - `disputes` → `Disputes`
>   - `motions` → `Motions`
>   - `objections` → `Objections`
> - hyphen inconsistency:
>   - `due-process` → `Due_Process`
> - spacing inconsistency:
>   - `STRATEGIC EVALUATION AND SETTLEMENT FRAMEWORK.md` → `STRATEGIC_EVALUATION_AND_SETTLEMENT_FRAMEWORK.md`
> 
> Rules:
> - preserve extensions
> - use underscores, no spaces
> - preserve known IDs and date prefixes
> - preserve exhibit IDs exactly
> - never alter top-level folder numbers
> - never rename anything inside `9_Archive` automatically
> 
> ### Pass C — duplicate detection
> Generate reports for likely duplicates:
> - same filename in different places
> - same stem with different extension
> - same size + same hash
> - same exhibit/video repeated at parent and child folder

> - `2_Data/exports/pre_import_cleanup/duplicate_candidates.csv`
> 
> Columns:
> - `duplicate_group`
> - `basis`
> - `file_path`
> - `filename`
> - `size_bytes`
> - `sha256`
> - `recommended_canonical_path`
> - `recommended_action`
>

> - `03_Pleadings/04_Service`

> - `promote_to_pleadings`
> - `promote_to_reference`

> # 5) My recommendation on the md-to-database truth
> 
> And yes — you were right earlier.
> 
> You absolutely have **too many markdown files that should really be rows**.
> 
> Good markdown:
> - narrative memos
> - legal arguments
> - long summaries
> - declarations
> - packet covers
> - theory writeups
> 
> Bad markdown:
> - one-line tasks
> - issue registers
> - event logs
> - actor directory
> - damages ledger
> - exhibit index
> - deadlines
> - strategy tracker
> 
> Those should be rows.
> 
> So the right move is:
> - keep narrative documents in markdown
> - convert structured repetitive records into database rows
> - keep the manifest as the bridge layer
> 
> That’s the grown-up architecture.
> 
> If you want, next I’ll give you the **Noco import mapping plan** from this manifest into your tables:
> `actors, events, issues, damages, tasks, phases, strategy, exhibits, filings, docs`.

### User
> ill give you the manifest, firs,t in teh mean time how can we pipe in our ai bot so it has presistenet context and rag, and also the ui for case management?

### Assistant
> Yes. And you should split this into **3 layers**, or it turns into spaghetti fast:
> 
> # The stack
> 
> ## 1. System of record
> This is your truth layer.
> 
> Use:
> - **NocoDB** for structured case data
> - **filesystem / Drive / repo** for source documents and evidence
> - **manifest table** as the bridge between files and records
> 
> So:
> 
> - Noco holds: actors, events, issues, damages, tasks, phases, exhibits, filings, strategy
> - files stay in repo / Drive
> - every file gets a manifest row
> - database rows can link back to `relative_path`, `sha256`, `doc_id`, `event_id`, etc.
> 
> That gives you persistent context without stuffing everything into prompts like a maniac.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## 2. RAG + AI memory layer
> This is your bot brain.
> 
> Use a pipeline like this:
> 
> ### A. Ingestion
> From repo + Noco:
> - markdown
> - PDFs after text extraction
> - transcripts
> - key CSV rows flattened to text
> - Noco records exported or fetched via MCP/API
> 
> ### B. Chunking
> Chunk by **record-aware boundaries**, not dumb 500-token chunks.
> 
> Examples:
> - one event = one chunk or a few chunks
> - one issue = one chunk
> - one filing = sections
> - one exhibit = one chunk plus metadata
> - one hearing transcript = page/section chunks
> 
> ### C. Embeddings store
> Use one of:
> - **pgvector on Postgres**
> - **Supabase pgvector**
> - local vector DB if you must, but Postgres is cleaner for this system
> 
> Best fit for you:
> - **Postgres + pgvector**
> Because then you can combine:
> - vector search
> - SQL filters
> - case metadata
> - access control
> - UI queries
> 
> ### D. Retrieval
> Bot retrieves by:
> - semantic similarity
> - metadata filters
> - case phase
> - issue type
> - filing status
> - evidence type
> - date range
> 
> So instead of “search everything,” you ask:
> - all chunks tagged `service_defect`
> - related to `C-01_Jurisdictional_Defects`
> - between `2025-05-01` and `2026-01-09`
> - linked to hearing prep
> 
> That is how you stop hallucinated mush.
> 
> ### E. Persistent memory
> Do **not** make the bot “remember everything” conversationally.
> 
> Instead create:
> - `conversation_threads`
> - `saved_research_notes`
> - `matter_context_profiles`
> - `working_sets`
> 
> Bot memory should be:
> - **session memory** = current chat thread
> - **working memory** = selected case bundle
> - **long-term memory** = Noco rows + vectorized docs + summaries
> 
> That’s the correct model.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended architecture for you
> 
> ## Backend
> You already have enough pieces to do this cleanly:
> 
> - **Frontend UI:** React/Vite
> - **API/backend:** FastAPI or Node/Express
> - **Database:** Postgres
> - **Structured app data:** NocoDB over Postgres, or Noco as ops UI over same DB
> - **Vector search:** pgvector
> - **File storage:** Drive/repo now, maybe object storage later
> - **Automation:** n8n or Python jobs
> - **LLM orchestration:** your own API layer, not buried inside UI
> 
> If you want the least stupid setup:
> 
> ### Best practical version
> - **Postgres** = single backend DB
> - **NocoDB** = admin/data-entry UI over selected tables
> - **FastAPI** = AI orchestration, retrieval, chat endpoints
> - **React case UI** = matter dashboard + search + timeline + tasking
> - **Python ingestion scripts** = manifest, extraction, embedding, sync
> - **pgvector** = RAG
> 
> That’s clean.
> 
> ---
> 
> # UI for case management
> 
> The UI should not be “chat first.”
> That’s where people build shiny garbage.
> 
> It should be **matter first, chat second**.
> 
> ## Main screens
> 
> ### 1. Case dashboard
> Show:
> - case summary
> - current phase
> - priority issues
> - deadlines
> - active tasks
> - recent filings
> - recent evidence added
> - regulator / court status
> - AI briefing panel
> 
> ### 2. Timeline view
> Show:
> - events
> - filings
> - service attempts
> - evidence captures
> - damages milestones
> 
> Filter by:
> - actor
> - phase
> - issue cluster
> - source type
> 
> ### 3. Issues board
> Each issue record should show:
> - issue title
> - theory cluster
> - facts linked
> - events linked
> - documents linked
> - exhibits linked
> - damages linked
> - current status
> - proof strength
> - related pleadings
> 
> ### 4. Evidence explorer
> Not just folders.
> 
> Should show:
> - document preview
> - source path
> - linked events/issues/exhibits
> - OCR/extracted text
> - tags
> - chain-of-custody fields
> - hash
> - court filed yes/no
> 
> ### 5. Pleadings / regulator workspace
> Show:
> - drafted
> - ready to file
> - filed
> - served
> - follow-up needed
> 
> ### 6. AI workspace
> This is where chat belongs.
> 
> Modes:
> - “brief this issue”
> - “build hearing packet”
> - “find contradictions”
> - “show missing proof”
> - “draft regulator complaint”
> - “prepare witness questions”
> 
> And the user must be able to pin a **working set**:
> - selected events
> - selected docs
> - selected issues
> - selected filings
> 
> That way AI works from a scoped bundle, not the whole planet.
> 
> ---
> 
> # How the bot should get context
> 
> Not by magic. By a retrieval pipeline.
> 
> ## Context assembly flow
> When you ask the bot something:
> 
> 1. user selects matter
> 2. system loads case profile
> 3. system loads current working set
> 4. system searches vector store
> 5. system also queries structured tables
> 6. system merges:
>    - case metadata
>    - selected rows
>    - top relevant chunks
>    - prior thread summary
> 7. only then build the prompt
> 
> So prompt context becomes:
> 
> - case summary
> - relevant actors
> - relevant issues
> - top 10 chunks
> - linked filings
> - user goal for this interaction
> 
> That’s the right shape.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Persistent context objects you should create
> 
> These are the tables or entities I’d add:
> 
> ## Core
> - `cases`
> - `actors`
> - `events`
> - `issues`
> - `damages`
> - `tasks`
> - `phases`
> - `filings`
> - `exhibits`
> - `documents`
> 
> ## AI / retrieval
> - `document_chunks`
> - `chunk_embeddings`
> - `conversation_threads`
> - `conversation_messages`
> - `thread_summaries`
> - `working_sets`
> - `working_set_items`
> - `saved_briefs`
> - `entity_mentions`
> - `retrieval_logs`
> 
> ## Bridge
> - `manifest`
> - `doc_event_links`
> - `doc_issue_links`
> - `event_issue_links`
> - `issue_damage_links`
> - `filing_exhibit_links`
> 
> That gives you a real case OS, not just a chatbot with vibes.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Simplest flow to get started now
> 
> ## Phase 1
> Use what you already have:
> - repo files
> - manifest CSV
> - Noco tables
> - Python scripts
> 
> Build:
> 
> ### Script 1: ingest_manifest_to_db
> Imports manifest into Postgres/Noco-backed tables
> 
> ### Script 2: extract_and_chunk
> Reads docs and outputs chunk rows
> 
> ### Script 3: embed_chunks
> Embeds chunk rows into pgvector
> 
> ### Script 4: query_case_context
> Given a question + case_id + filters, returns:
> - top rows
> - top chunks
> - summarized context pack
> 
> ### API
> Endpoints like:
> - `/cases/:id/dashboard`
> - `/cases/:id/timeline`
> - `/cases/:id/issues`
> - `/cases/:id/evidence`
> - `/cases/:id/ai/query`
> 
> ### Frontend
> Tabs:
> - Dashboard
> - Timeline
> - Issues
> - Evidence
> - Pleadings
> - AI
> 
> That’s enough to start without overbuilding.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What not to do
> 
> Do not:
> - store all context only in chat history
> - rely only on vector search with no metadata filtering
> - let AI mutate records directly without review
> - dump every markdown file into embeddings with zero structure
> - make the UI only a chatbot
> - use Noco as the entire product UI if you want serious case management
> 
> Noco is good as an admin/data layer.
> It is not the final polished operator UI.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best practical answer to your actual question
> 
> Yes, you can pipe in your AI bot with persistent context by using:
> 
> - **Noco/Postgres for structured case records**
> - **manifest + extracted text for document intelligence**
> - **pgvector for RAG**
> - **FastAPI orchestration layer**
> - **React case management UI**
> - **working-set based retrieval so the bot stays scoped**
> 
> That gives you:
> - persistent memory
> - case-aware search
> - structured legal workflow
> - custom UI
> - AI that can brief, draft, and analyze without losing the plot
> 
> # Next move
> 
> Send the manifest next, and I’ll map it into:
> 1. **database schema**
> 2. **RAG ingestion flow**
> 3. **screen-by-screen UI layout**
> 4. **the exact first API endpoints to build**

### User
> {'asset_pointer': 'sediment://file_0000000048f471fdb79f9a81893aa250', 'content_type': 'image_asset_pointer', 'fovea': None, 'height': 886, 'metadata': {'asset_pointer_link': None, 'container_pixel_height': None, 'container_pixel_width': None, 'dalle': None, 'emu_omit_glimpse_image': None, 'emu_patches_override': None, 'generation': None, 'gizmo': None, 'is_no_auth_placeholder': None, 'lpe_delta_encoding_channel': None, 'lpe_keep_patch_ijhw': None, 'sanitized': True, 'watermarked_asset_pointer': None}, 'size_bytes': 70678, 'width': 892}well since this will not be so large now that we are moving many of the md files to a row why cant we go ahead and host the pdf and image in a volume or something its less than one gb and we can compress the pdf and images and videos but keep the og copy on the drive for just in case. or is that doing too much i don't see a easy way of linking them back to good drive its usually a pain, I would need to use sometime of bucket and its better that way we can run the ocr on then in railway too and like i said we can keep the original photos and videos and pdfs on the drive as back up. now that you know that give me the best railway setup to add to our noco db bundle we have currently.

### Assistant
> Yes — storing the **working copy** of PDFs/images on Railway is reasonable **for this case**, as long as you treat Drive as the cold archive and Railway as the hot operational layer.
> 
> That is not overbuilding. It is actually cleaner.
> 
> The only thing I would **not** put on a Railway volume as your primary media layer is a growing, app-facing file store long term. Railway volumes are persistent and backed up, but they are attached to services and have plan-based size limits. Railway now also has private S3-compatible Buckets, which are a better fit for app file storage than shoving everything into a mounted disk. Volumes start at 0.5 GB on Free/Trial, 5 GB on Hobby, and 50 GB on Pro, with higher limits available; Buckets are designed for durable object storage and are accessed with credentials, presigned URLs, or via your backend. citeturn255771search0turn255771search9turn255771search11
> 
> So the clean answer is:
> 
> ## Best setup for your Railway project
> 
> Keep your current:
> - **NocoDB**
> - **Postgres**
> - **Redis**
> 
> Add these:
> 
> - **API service** for your app and AI orchestration
> - **Worker service** for OCR, ingestion, embeddings, background jobs
> - **Railway Bucket** for hot case files
> - optional **frontend service** if you want the custom case UI hosted there too
> 
> That gives you this shape:
> 
> ### 1. Postgres
> Use as the real system database:
> - case tables
> - manifest tables
> - app tables
> - AI memory tables
> - chunk metadata
> - file metadata
> - links between records and files
> 
> Also install/use **pgvector** in Postgres for embeddings and retrieval.
> 
> ### 2. NocoDB
> Use as your ops/admin layer:
> - manual review
> - bulk edits
> - quick filtering
> - link records
> - internal data maintenance
> 
> Do **not** make Noco the final user-facing case app.
> 
> ### 3. Redis
> Keep it for:
> - job queue
> - OCR tasks
> - embedding jobs
> - thumbnail generation
> - long-running imports
> - chat cache / retrieval cache if needed
> 
> ### 4. API service
> This is the brain and gateway.
> 
> Use **FastAPI** if you want the least friction with Python OCR/RAG tooling.
> 
> It should handle:
> - auth
> - case dashboard APIs
> - file lookup
> - presigned file access or proxy access
> - RAG query assembly
> - chat endpoints
> - sync with Noco/Postgres
> - manifest ingestion triggers
> 
> ### 5. Worker service
> Separate from the API.
> 
> This service should:
> - ingest files
> - extract text from PDFs
> - OCR image PDFs and photos
> - create thumbnails/previews
> - chunk text
> - embed chunks
> - update manifest/file tables
> - mark processing status
> 
> Keeping this separate prevents your API from choking every time you import a fat evidence batch.
> 
> ### 6. Railway Bucket
> This is the part you were circling around, and yes, it’s the right move.
> 
> Use the bucket for:
> - compressed working PDFs
> - normalized JPG/PNG derivatives
> - OCR-ready copies
> - thumbnails
> - transcript exports
> - maybe optimized MP4 proxies later
> 
> Keep original raw files in Drive as archive.
> 
> That gives you:
> - easier app linking
> - consistent URLs/keys
> - easier OCR pipeline
> - easier preview generation
> - less Drive API nonsense
> 
> Railway Buckets are private and S3-compatible, which is exactly what you want for controlled app access. citeturn255771search9
> 
> ---
> 
> # What I would **not** do
> 
> I would **not** use only a mounted volume for your operational file layer unless this is purely temporary.
> 
> Why:
> - volumes are better for service-local state and processing scratch
> - buckets are better for object-style case assets
> - app code linking to object keys is cleaner than linking to random file paths in a service mount
> 
> Use a volume for:
> - temp OCR workspace
> - local processing cache
> - intermediate files
> - maybe model cache if needed
> 
> Use a bucket for:
> - actual working evidence assets
> 
> That’s the right split.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best Railway architecture for your exact case
> 
> ## Minimal serious production stack
> 
> ### Existing
> - **NocoDB service**
> - **Postgres service**
> - **Redis service**
> 
> ### Add
> - **fcfcu-api**
> - **fcfcu-worker**
> - **fcfcu-files bucket**
> - optional **fcfcu-web**
> 
> ---
> 
> # Recommended responsibilities
> 
> ## fcfcu-api
> Tech:
> - FastAPI
> - SQLAlchemy
> - background job submission to Redis queue
> - S3 client for Railway Bucket
> - OpenAI-compatible orchestration layer or whatever model provider you choose
> 
> Endpoints:
> - `/cases`
> - `/cases/{id}/dashboard`
> - `/cases/{id}/timeline`
> - `/cases/{id}/issues`
> - `/cases/{id}/evidence`
> - `/cases/{id}/chat`
> - `/files/{id}/url`
> - `/ingest/manifest`
> - `/search`
> 
> ## fcfcu-worker
> Tech:
> - Python
> - Celery/RQ/Arq/Dramatiq, whatever queue tool you prefer
> - OCR tools
> - PDF text extraction
> - ffmpeg for media derivatives
> - embedding pipeline
> 
> Jobs:
> - `ingest_file`
> - `extract_text`
> - `ocr_document`
> - `generate_thumbnail`
> - `chunk_document`
> - `embed_document`
> - `sync_manifest_row`
> - `rebuild_case_context`
> 
> ## fcfcu-files bucket
> Structure like:
> - `cases/fcfcu/original_working/...`
> - `cases/fcfcu/derivatives/...`
> - `cases/fcfcu/thumbnails/...`
> - `cases/fcfcu/ocr/...`
> - `cases/fcfcu/transcripts/...`
> 
> And in Postgres store:
> - `storage_provider`
> - `bucket_name`
> - `object_key`
> - `original_drive_path`
> - `sha256`
> - `mime_type`
> - `size_bytes`
> - `derivative_of`
> - `processing_status`
> 
> ---
> 
> # Your source-of-truth model
> 
> Use this exact hierarchy mentally:
> 
> ### Cold archive
> **Google Drive**
> - untouched originals
> - long-term backup
> - “oh shit” recovery source
> 
> ### Hot operational store
> **Railway Bucket**
> - app-facing working copies
> - compressed/normalized assets
> - OCR and preview targets
> 
> ### Processing scratch
> **Railway Volume attached to worker**
> - temp files
> - OCR staging
> - decompression/recompression
> - ffmpeg work area
> 
> ### Structured metadata
> **Postgres + Noco**
> - everything relational
> 
> That is the clean model.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Why this is better than Drive linking directly
> 
> Because direct Drive integration usually becomes annoying in exactly the ways you already know:
> - auth friction
> - weird share links
> - unstable preview behavior
> - ugly URLs
> - poor app integration
> - painful background processing
> - messy access control
> 
> With a bucket:
> - your app controls the file layer
> - OCR jobs can read directly
> - you can store normalized derivatives
> - linking is stable
> - later UI is easier
> 
> That’s worth it.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Compression strategy
> 
> Your instinct is right, but do not get reckless.
> 
> ## Keep in Railway hot store:
> - normalized PDFs
> - preview JPG/PNG
> - compressed working images
> - lower-res video proxies only if needed
> 
> ## Keep only in Drive archive:
> - raw phone originals
> - full-quality HEIC originals
> - giant original MP4s if they are too heavy
> 
> For many legal workflows, you do **not** need every giant original video in Railway to start. Keep:
> - metadata row in DB
> - transcript / extracted notes
> - proxy if needed
> - original pointer to Drive
> 
> That avoids bloating your infra for no reason.
> 
> ---
> 
> # My recommendation for you, bluntly
> 
> ## Do this now
> Add:
> - **1 API service**
> - **1 worker service**
> - **1 Railway Bucket**
> 
> Keep:
> - NocoDB
> - Postgres
> - Redis
> 
> Use:
> - Bucket = working evidence
> - Drive = archive originals
> - Worker volume = temp processing only
> 
> ## Do not do this now
> - do not build a giant multi-service media platform
> - do not self-host MinIO unless you have a specific reason
> - do not cram all evidence into a Noco attachment field and call it architecture
> - do not rely on local-only repo paths as your app file system
> 
> Railway Buckets make the MinIO detour unnecessary for your size and use case. Railway’s own bucket product is already S3-compatible and purpose-built for project storage. citeturn255771search9turn255771search3
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best add-ons to your current bundle
> 
> If you’re asking what to literally add on the Railway canvas, add this:
> 
> 1. **FastAPI service**  
>    `fcfcu-api`
> 
> 2. **Python worker service**  
>    `fcfcu-worker`
> 
> 3. **Bucket**  
>    `fcfcu-files`
> 
> Optional fourth:
> 4. **React frontend**  
>    `fcfcu-web`
> 
> That is the best next step.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Suggested internal data flow
> 
> 1. file enters from repo/Drive/manual upload  
> 2. worker copies or creates normalized working asset in bucket  
> 3. metadata row created in Postgres  
> 4. OCR/text extraction runs  
> 5. chunks + embeddings stored in Postgres  
> 6. Noco shows linked row data  
> 7. API assembles dashboard / search / AI context  
> 8. frontend displays matter UI and previews
> 
> That’s your real case OS spine.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Decision
> 
> **Yes, use Railway-hosted hot storage.**  
> But use a **Bucket for operational assets** and a **Volume only for processing scratch**, while keeping Drive as archival backup.
> 
> That is the clean version, not the hacky version.
> 
> Send the manifest next and I’ll turn this into:
> - exact service names
> - env var map
> - bucket key convention
> - Postgres tables for files/chunks
> - first-pass deployment order

### User
> they have a lot of options for api, alls of that, so can you be more specific.

### Assistant
> Yep. Here’s the **specific Railway setup**, not the vague “add an API” fluff.
> 
> ## What to add on Railway canvas
> 
> You already have:
> - **NocoDB**
> - **Postgres**
> - **Redis**
> 
> Add exactly these next:
> 
> ### 1. `fcfcu-api`
> Create an **empty service** or deploy from your repo if you already have one. Railway supports adding an empty service, GitHub repo, Docker image, or database. citeturn268918search0
> 
> **Purpose**
> - backend for your case app
> - auth
> - file metadata
> - search endpoint
> - AI chat endpoint
> - signed file URLs
> - dashboard data for UI
> 
> **Use**
> - Python **FastAPI**
> - public domain attached
> - no volume needed
> 
> ### 2. `fcfcu-worker`
> Create another **empty service** or repo/image service. citeturn268918search0
> 
> **Purpose**
> - OCR jobs
> - PDF text extraction
> - image normalization
> - chunking + embeddings
> - transcript generation
> - manifest import jobs
> 
> **Use**
> - Python worker
> - no public domain needed
> - attach **one volume** to this service only
> 
> ### 3. `fcfcu-files` bucket
> Create a **Bucket** from the Railway canvas. Buckets are private, S3-compatible object storage, and each environment gets its own isolated instance and credentials. citeturn268918search3
> 
> **Purpose**
> - working PDFs
> - normalized images
> - thumbnails
> - OCR-ready copies
> - transcript exports
> - maybe smaller video proxies later
> 
> ### 4. Optional: `fcfcu-web`
> Only if you want the custom case UI hosted on Railway too.
> 
> **Purpose**
> - React/Vite frontend
> - dashboards
> - case timeline
> - evidence browser
> - AI chat panel
> 
> If you are not ready for frontend yet, skip it for now.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Exact role of each thing
> 
> ## Postgres
> This is your **real database**.
> 
> Use it for:
> - files
> - chunks
> - embeddings
> - issues
> - actors
> - damages
> - phases
> - tasks
> - strategy notes
> - evidence links
> - chat sessions
> - retrieval logs
> 
> Also: enable/use **pgvector** if your Postgres image supports it, because that is the cleanest place to keep embeddings.
> 
> ## NocoDB
> This is your **admin panel / operations desk**, not the final app.
> 
> Use it for:
> - manual data entry
> - fixing rows
> - linking records
> - reviewing imports
> - quick filters and views
> 
> ## Redis
> Use it as the **job queue / fast cache**. Railway’s Redis template exposes standard connection env vars like `REDISHOST`, `REDISPORT`, `REDISPASSWORD`, and `REDIS_URL`. citeturn268918search4
> 
> Use it for:
> - queued OCR jobs
> - embedding jobs
> - manifest processing
> - cache retrieval results
> - rate limiting if needed
> 
> ## Bucket
> This is your **hot file layer**. Railway Buckets are private, use S3 credentials, and are best accessed via presigned URLs or your backend. citeturn268918search3turn268918search5
> 
> Use it for:
> - app-facing evidence files
> - optimized working copies
> - derivatives
> 
> ## Worker volume
> Use the volume as **scratch workspace only**.
> 
> Railway volumes are persistent, but each service can only have one volume, replicas cannot be used with volumes, and redeploys on volume-backed services can briefly interrupt service. citeturn268918search1
> 
> So:
> - attach volume to worker
> - do temp OCR there
> - do ffmpeg there
> - do conversion there
> - then push final outputs into the bucket
> 
> Do **not** make the volume your long-term evidence library.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best architecture for your case
> 
> ## Recommended stack
> 
> ### Service: `nocodb`
> - keep as-is
> - internal only if possible
> 
> ### Service: `postgres`
> - keep as-is
> - internal only
> 
> ### Service: `redis`
> - keep as-is
> - internal only
> 
> ### Service: `fcfcu-api`
> - FastAPI
> - public domain
> - talks to Postgres, Redis, Bucket
> - talks to Noco only if needed
> 
> ### Service: `fcfcu-worker`
> - Python worker
> - no public domain
> - mounted volume at something like `/workspace`
> - talks to Postgres, Redis, Bucket
> 
> ### Resource: `fcfcu-files`
> - Railway Bucket
> - private
> - credentials passed to api + worker using variable references Railway supports for buckets like `BUCKET`, `ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`, `ENDPOINT`, and `REGION`. citeturn268918search3
> 
> ### Optional service: `fcfcu-web`
> - React/Vite frontend
> - public domain
> - only talks to `fcfcu-api`
> 
> ---
> 
> # Concrete bucket and volume usage
> 
> ## Bucket
> Use object keys like:
> 
> ```text
> cases/fcfcu/original_working/pdf/
> cases/fcfcu/derived/images/
> cases/fcfcu/derived/thumbs/
> cases/fcfcu/ocr/
> cases/fcfcu/transcripts/
> cases/fcfcu/video_proxy/
> ```
> 
> ## Worker volume
> Mount path:
> 
> ```text
> /workspace
> ```
> 
> Inside it use:
> 
> ```text
> /workspace/inbox
> /workspace/tmp
> /workspace/ocr
> /workspace/render
> /workspace/ffmpeg
> /workspace/logs
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # What service type to choose in Railway
> 
> You said they show lots of API/options. So here’s the direct call:
> 
> ## For `fcfcu-api`
> Choose:
> - **Empty Service** if you’re starting clean
> - or **GitHub Repo service** if you already have backend code
> - or **Docker image** only if you want tighter deployment control
> 
> Best practical move: **empty service tied to your repo later** or straight from repo. Railway supports both. citeturn268918search0
> 
> ## For `fcfcu-worker`
> Choose:
> - **Empty Service** or **GitHub Repo**
> - same repo is fine if monorepo
> - separate start command from the API
> 
> ## For files
> Choose:
> - **Bucket**, not database, not volume, not another service
> 
> ## For scratch disk
> Choose:
> - **Volume attached to worker**
> 
> ---
> 
> # Internal networking setup
> 
> Railway gives each service a private internal DNS name like:
> 
> ```text
> <service-name>.railway.internal
> ```
> 
> That traffic stays on Railway’s private network. citeturn268918search6turn268918search8
> 
> So use:
> 
> - `postgres.railway.internal`
> - `redis.railway.internal`
> - `fcfcu-api.railway.internal`
> - `nocodb.railway.internal`
> 
> Your frontend should **not** talk directly to Postgres, Redis, Noco, or bucket. Only to `fcfcu-api`.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Exact deployment recommendation
> 
> ## Option A — best for you now
> One repo, two services:
> - `fcfcu-api`
> - `fcfcu-worker`
> 
> Both from the same monorepo, different start commands.
> 
> Example:
> 
> ### `fcfcu-api`
> Start command:
> ```bash
> uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT
> ```
> 
> ### `fcfcu-worker`
> Start command:
> ```bash
> python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> This is cleaner than making ten microservices like a clown.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Environment variables to set
> 
> ## Shared on API + Worker
> 
> ```env
> APP_ENV=production
> 
> DATABASE_URL=${{Postgres.DATABASE_URL}}
> REDIS_URL=${{Redis.REDIS_URL}}
> 
> S3_ENDPOINT=${{fcfcu-files.ENDPOINT}}
> S3_REGION=${{fcfcu-files.REGION}}
> S3_BUCKET=${{fcfcu-files.BUCKET}}
> S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID=${{fcfcu-files.ACCESS_KEY_ID}}
> S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=${{fcfcu-files.SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}}
> 
> CASE_ID=fcfcu
> CASE_SLUG=30.06_CASE_FCFCU
> ```
> 
> Bucket variable references are supported directly by Railway. citeturn268918search3
> 
> ## Worker-only
> ```env
> WORKDIR=/workspace
> OCR_TMP_DIR=/workspace/ocr
> FFMPEG_TMP_DIR=/workspace/ffmpeg
> INGEST_TMP_DIR=/workspace/inbox
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> # File serving pattern
> 
> Do **not** serve files by streaming all of them through your API unless needed.
> 
> Best pattern:
> 1. frontend asks API for file
> 2. API checks permissions
> 3. API returns **presigned URL**
> 4. browser downloads directly from bucket
> 
> Railway specifically recommends presigned URLs as a way to avoid service egress costs, because files come directly from the bucket. citeturn268918search5
> 
> That matters.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Cost-smart version
> 
> Because your case data is under 1 GB, this stack is fine.
> 
> But keep this distinction straight:
> 
> ## Keep in bucket
> - normalized PDFs
> - JPG derivatives
> - thumbnails
> - transcripts
> - maybe compressed proxy MP4s
> 
> ## Keep only in Drive
> - raw originals
> - full-res HEIC
> - giant raw videos
> - anything you rarely need in app
> 
> This keeps Railway useful without becoming your junk drawer.
> 
> ---
> 
> # What not to add right now
> 
> Do **not** add:
> - MinIO
> - another Postgres
> - another Redis
> - separate OCR microservice
> - separate vector DB
> - Elasticsearch
> - RabbitMQ
> 
> You do not need that mess yet.
> 
> Your current stack plus:
> - API
> - Worker
> - Bucket
> - one worker volume
> 
> is enough.
> 
> ---
> 
> # The exact Railway canvas I would build
> 
> ```text
> NocoDB
> Postgres
> Redis
> fcfcu-api
> fcfcu-worker
> fcfcu-files (Bucket)
> [optional] fcfcu-web
> [attached to worker] Volume mounted at /workspace
> ```
> 
> That’s the setup.
> 
> ---
> 
> # Best next click order in Railway
> 
> 1. Add **Bucket** → name it `fcfcu-files`
> 2. Add **Empty Service** → `fcfcu-api`
> 3. Add **Empty Service** → `fcfcu-worker`
> 4. Attach **Volume** to `fcfcu-worker` at `/workspace`
> 5. Wire bucket credentials into `fcfcu-api` and `fcfcu-worker`
> 6. Wire `DATABASE_URL` and `REDIS_URL`
> 7. Deploy API first
> 8. Deploy worker second
> 9. Add frontend later
> 
> ---
> 
> # My blunt recommendation
> 
> For **your exact use case**, the best Railway add-ons are:
> 
> - **1 Bucket**
> - **1 FastAPI service**
> - **1 Python worker**
> - **1 worker volume**
> 
> That’s it.
> 
> If you want, next I’ll give you the **exact FastAPI + worker folder structure**, the **Railway service names/env map**, and the **Postgres tables for files/chunks/chat memory** so you can build it without guessing.

### User
> you need to be more specif on teh python worker there is no such thingk. and what would the mount path be for the volumne.

### Assistant
> Yep — you’re right to call that out. There is **no special Railway service type called “Python worker.”** Railway just has **services**; a service is a container deployment target, and you make it behave like a worker by what code you deploy and what **start command** you give it. Railway’s docs describe services as generic deployment targets and say you can create them from a GitHub repo, local directory, Docker image, or empty service, then set a custom start command. citeturn705199search1turn705199search3turn705199search4turn705199search7
> 
> So the concrete answer is:
> 
> ## What to create
> 
> Create **one regular service** named something like:
> 
> - `fcfcu-api`
> - `fcfcu-worker`
> 
> The difference is not the service type. The difference is the **start command**.
> 
> Example:
> 
> ### `fcfcu-api`
> Runs your web backend:
> ```bash
> uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT
> ```
> 
> ### `fcfcu-worker`
> Runs your background processor:
> ```bash
> python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> That is a normal Railway service with a Python process as its start command. Railway explicitly supports overriding the start command for a service. citeturn705199search4turn705199search7
> 
> ## What “worker” means here
> 
> It just means:
> 
> - no public UI needed
> - no browser traffic
> - it pulls jobs from Redis or polls Postgres
> - it processes OCR / PDFs / thumbnails / embeddings
> - it writes results back to Postgres and your storage layer
> 
> So “worker” is an **architecture role**, not a Railway product label.
> 
> ## What mount path to use for the volume
> 
> Use:
> 
> ```text
> /workspace
> ```
> 
> That’s the cleanest choice.
> 
> Why:
> - Railway volumes are mounted at **whatever absolute path you specify**
> - the mount path becomes available inside the container as a normal directory
> - Railway also exposes `RAILWAY_VOLUME_MOUNT_PATH` automatically at runtime
> - if you want the volume inside your app folder specifically, Railway notes that Nixpacks usually puts app code in `/app`, so a path like `/app/data` is valid too citeturn705199search2turn705199search5
> 
> So you have two sane choices:
> 
> ### Best general-purpose choice
> ```text
> /workspace
> ```
> 
> ### Best if your code expects relative app paths like `./data`
> ```text
> /app/data
> ```
> 
> ## My recommendation for your case
> 
> Use:
> 
> ```text
> /workspace
> ```
> 
> Then inside that volume, your worker uses subfolders like:
> 
> ```text
> /workspace/inbox
> /workspace/tmp
> /workspace/ocr
> /workspace/render
> /workspace/thumbs
> /workspace/transcripts
> /workspace/logs
> ```
> 
> That avoids mixing persistent scratch files with deployed app code.
> 
> ## What to click in Railway
> 
> For the worker:
> 
> 1. Click **Add Service**
> 2. Choose **Empty Service** or connect your **GitHub repo**
> 3. Name it `fcfcu-worker`
> 4. Set the start command to:
>    ```bash
>    python -m app.worker
>    ```
> 5. Attach a volume
> 6. Set the volume mount path to:
>    ```text
>    /workspace
>    ```
> 
> Railway’s docs say volumes are attached to a service and you configure the mount path yourself; if you mount to `/foobar`, your app accesses it at `/foobar`. citeturn705199search2turn705199search0
> 
> ## Best setup for you, corrected and specific
> 
> ### Service 1: `fcfcu-api`
> - type: regular Railway service
> - source: GitHub repo or empty service
> - start command:
>   ```bash
>   uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT
>   ```
> - no volume needed
> 
> ### Service 2: `fcfcu-worker`
> - type: regular Railway service
> - source: same repo is fine
> - start command:
>   ```bash
>   python -m app.worker
>   ```
> - attach volume at:
>   ```text
>   /workspace
>   ```
> 
> ### Storage
> - use a Railway **Bucket** for app-facing files if you want S3-style object storage
> - use the worker volume only as scratch / processing / temporary persistent work area citeturn705199search2turn705199search1
> 
> ## Blunt answer
> 
> You do **not** need to hunt for some special “Python worker” option because it does not exist.  
> You need a **normal service** whose start command runs a Python worker process.
> 
> And the volume mount path should be:
> 
> ```text
> /workspace
> ```
> 
> unless your code specifically expects a relative folder under the app root, in which case use:
> 
> ```text
> /app/data
> ```
> 
> Next move: I can give you the exact **repo structure + Railway service settings + worker `app.worker` starter file** so you can paste it in without guessing.

### Assistant
> This is a lot closer. The shape is basically right.
> 
> ## What you have now
> 
> Good:
> - `Postgres` for NocoDB + app metadata
> - `Redis` for jobs/queue
> - `fcfcu-api` for your backend
> - `fcfcu-worker` for OCR/ingest/processing
> - `fcfcu-files` bucket for PDFs/images/assets
> - React frontend separate from API
> 
> That is the **correct direction** for your use case. Railway’s current docs support exactly this split: services for app processes, volumes for persistent service-local data, and buckets for S3-compatible object storage. citeturn456462search0turn456462search1turn456462search4turn456462search5
> 
> ## The one thing I’d change
> 
> Do **not** treat the worker volume as your primary file library.
> 
> Use the bucket as the primary app file store. Use the worker volume as **scratch space only**.
> 
> Reason:
> - Volumes are mounted to a single service path and are best for persistent service-local data. citeturn456462search1turn456462search3
> - Buckets are the right fit for PDFs, images, uploads, derivatives, and app-served files because they are durable S3-compatible object storage. citeturn456462search0turn456462search6
> 
> So your pattern should be:
> 
> - **Google Drive** = original source / cold archive / backup
> - **Railway Bucket (`fcfcu-files`)** = app-facing canonical working copies
> - **Worker volume** = temp OCR, render, convert, unzip, thumbnail, ffmpeg workspace
> - **Postgres** = metadata, links, case records, manifests, file registry
> - **Redis** = job queue only
> 
> ## Best concrete Railway setup
> 
> ### 1. `admin-panel-nocodb`
> Keep as-is.
> 
> Purpose:
> - internal case ops admin
> - table editing
> - light file metadata review
> 
> Volume:
> - keep the Noco volume attached to Noco only
> 
> Do **not** try to make Noco itself your file processor.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 2. `Postgres`
> Keep as-is.
> 
> Purpose:
> - NocoDB backend
> - app metadata
> - file registry
> - embeddings metadata if you want
> - case records, issues, exhibits, tasks, phases
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 3. `Jobs - Redis`
> Keep as-is.
> 
> Purpose:
> - queue only
> 
> Use for jobs like:
> - `extract_text`
> - `ocr_pdf`
> - `make_thumbnail`
> - `transcribe_audio`
> - `ingest_manifest_row`
> - `rebuild_embeddings`
> - `sync_drive_stub`
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 4. `fcfcu-files` bucket
> Yes, keep this.
> 
> This should become your app’s primary file object store.
> 
> Use it for:
> - normalized PDFs
> - compressed images
> - generated thumbnails
> - OCR JSON/text sidecars
> - transcript text
> - exhibit bundles
> - preview assets
> 
> Buckets are private and S3-compatible, and Railway recommends presigned URLs or backend proxying to serve files. citeturn456462search0turn456462search6
> 
> ### Recommended bucket folder/key pattern
> Use object keys like this:
> 
> ```text
> cases/fcfcu/original/pdf/2026-02-03_C03_FCRA_TRNUN_DISP_D0498.pdf
> cases/fcfcu/derived/thumbs/2026-02-03_C03_FCRA_TRNUN_DISP_D0498/page-1.jpg
> cases/fcfcu/derived/ocr/2026-02-03_C03_FCRA_TRNUN_DISP_D0498.json
> cases/fcfcu/derived/text/2026-02-03_C03_FCRA_TRNUN_DISP_D0498.txt
> cases/fcfcu/photos/2025-12-09/EX-V-09-143_2025-12-09_0945.jpg
> cases/fcfcu/video/2025-12-17/EX-V-17-807_2025-12-17_1357.mp4
> cases/fcfcu/exhibits/final/EX-Q_Tradeline_Excerpts.pdf
> ```
> 
> That is way cleaner than trying to mirror every nested Drive folder exactly.
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 5. `fcfcu-worker`
> This is the one that matters most.
> 
> This should be a normal Railway service with a Python start command override. Railway supports custom start commands directly. citeturn456462search5
> 
> #### Mount path
> Use:
> 
> ```text
> /workspace
> ```
> 
> Railway says the mount path you specify becomes available at that absolute path, and if you need a relative app path you’d use `/app/...`. citeturn456462search1
> 
> For you, `/workspace` is better.
> 
> #### Use the volume for:
> ```text
> /workspace/inbox
> /workspace/tmp
> /workspace/ocr
> /workspace/render
> /workspace/transcode
> /workspace/thumbs
> /workspace/logs
> ```
> 
> #### Do not store your permanent case files only there.
> That would be a design mistake.
> 
> #### Worker start command
> ```bash
> python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> or if you use Celery/RQ:
> ```bash
> python -m app.jobs.runner
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 6. `fcfcu-api`
> Good.
> 
> This should do:
> - auth
> - case record APIs
> - file metadata APIs
> - presigned upload/download URL generation
> - trigger ingest jobs
> - search endpoint
> - chat endpoint
> - Noco/Postgres reads and writes
> 
> Start command:
> ```bash
> uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT
> ```
> 
> Railway supports overriding the start command for services. citeturn456462search5
> 
> ---
> 
> ### 7. React frontend
> Good.
> 
> This should not talk directly to Redis, Postgres, or the bucket.
> It should talk to `fcfcu-api`.
> 
> ## What the actual data flow should be
> 
> ### Upload / ingest flow
> 1. File uploaded from frontend or imported from Drive
> 2. API writes object to `fcfcu-files`
> 3. API creates `file_asset` / `document` / `evidence_item` record in Postgres
> 4. API pushes Redis job
> 5. Worker downloads object from bucket to `/workspace/tmp`
> 6. Worker OCRs / converts / thumbnails / extracts text
> 7. Worker writes outputs back to bucket
> 8. Worker updates Postgres metadata rows
> 9. UI reads finished status from API
> 
> That’s the clean version.
> 
> ## What you should add next on Railway
> 
> Your current bundle is missing only one real thing conceptually:
> 
> ### Add environment variables from the bucket into:
> - `fcfcu-api`
> - `fcfcu-worker`
> 
> You want these available in both:
> - `ACCESS_KEY_ID`
> - `SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`
> - `BUCKET`
> - `ENDPOINT`
> - `REGION`
> 
> Railway says buckets expose these variables for other services via variable references. citeturn456462search0
> 
> ## What I would not add yet
> 
> Do **not** add:
> - Elasticsearch
> - separate vector DB
> - MinIO
> - extra Postgres instance
> - another OCR microservice
> - another storage provider
> 
> That’s premature architecture cosplay.
> 
> For under 1 GB and a single case system, this stack is enough:
> - NocoDB
> - Postgres
> - Redis
> - Bucket
> - API
> - Worker
> - Frontend
> 
> ## Best naming cleanup
> 
> Your canvas is understandable, but I’d tighten names a bit:
> 
> - `admin-panel-nocodb` → `fcfcu-nocodb`
> - `Jobs - Redis` → `fcfcu-redis`
> - `fcfcu-files` → keep
> - `fcfcu-worker` → keep
> - `fcfcu-api` → keep
> - `React Frontend` → `fcfcu-web`
> 
> Cleaner, less fluffy.
> 
> ## Final verdict
> 
> Your setup is now **structurally right**.
> 
> The only architectural correction is this:
> 
> **Bucket = permanent working files**  
> **Worker volume = temporary processing space**
> 
> Not the other way around.
> 
> That’s the piece that keeps this sane.
> 
> Here’s the exact target setup in one shot:
> 
> ```text
> Railway Project: fcfcu-ops
> 
> 1. fcfcu-nocodb
>    - NocoDB admin panel
>    - attached Noco volume
> 
> 2. postgres
>    - primary database
> 
> 3. fcfcu-redis
>    - queue / cache
> 
> 4. fcfcu-files
>    - Railway bucket
>    - canonical app file storage
> 
> 5. fcfcu-api
>    - FastAPI backend
>    - start: uvicorn app.main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT
>    - env refs to postgres, redis, bucket
> 
> 6. fcfcu-worker
>    - Python worker
>    - start: python -m app.worker
>    - attached volume mounted at /workspace
>    - env refs to postgres, redis, bucket
> 
> 7. fcfcu-web
>    - React frontend
>    - calls API only
> ```
> 
> Next step should be either the exact env var map for each Railway service, or the starter repo structure for `fcfcu-api` + `fcfcu-worker`.

### User
> this is my fact api https://github.com/railwayapp-templates/fastapi.git and i added a new repor for my worker just need the code for that.

### Assistant
> Yep. Since your API is already the Railway FastAPI template, the worker should be its own tiny Python service with one job loop, Redis queue, bucket access, and scratch volume at `/workspace`.
> 
> Use this exact starter for the **worker repo**.
> 
> ## Repo shape
> 
> ```text
> fcfcu-worker/
> ├─ app/
> │  ├─ __init__.py
> │  ├─ config.py
> │  ├─ storage.py
> │  ├─ jobs.py
> │  └─ worker.py
> ├─ requirements.txt
> ├─ Procfile
> ├─ .env.example
> └─ README.md
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `requirements.txt`
> 
> ```txt
> boto3==1.35.99
> botocore==1.35.99
> redis==5.0.8
> rq==1.16.2
> pypdf==5.1.0
> python-dotenv==1.0.1
> psycopg[binary]==3.2.3
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `Procfile`
> 
> ```txt
> worker: python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> On Railway, set the service start command to:
> 
> ```bash
> python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `.env.example`
> 
> ```env
> REDIS_URL=redis://default:password@redis:6379
> DATABASE_URL=postgresql://postgres:password@postgres:5432/postgres
> 
> S3_ENDPOINT_URL=
> S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID=
> S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=
> S3_BUCKET=fcfcu-files
> S3_REGION=us-east-1
> 
> WORKSPACE_DIR=/workspace
> LOG_LEVEL=INFO
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `app/config.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import logging
> import os
> from dataclasses import dataclass
> 
> 
> @dataclass(frozen=True)
> class Settings:
>     redis_url: str
>     database_url: str
>     s3_endpoint_url: str | None
>     s3_access_key_id: str | None
>     s3_secret_access_key: str | None
>     s3_bucket: str
>     s3_region: str | None
>     workspace_dir: str
>     log_level: str
> 
>     @staticmethod
>     def from_env() -> "Settings":
>         return Settings(
>             redis_url=os.environ["REDIS_URL"],
>             database_url=os.environ["DATABASE_URL"],
>             s3_endpoint_url=os.getenv("S3_ENDPOINT_URL"),
>             s3_access_key_id=os.getenv("S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID"),
>             s3_secret_access_key=os.getenv("S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY"),
>             s3_bucket=os.environ["S3_BUCKET"],
>             s3_region=os.getenv("S3_REGION"),
>             workspace_dir=os.getenv("WORKSPACE_DIR", "/workspace"),
>             log_level=os.getenv("LOG_LEVEL", "INFO"),
>         )
> 
> 
> def configure_logging(level: str) -> None:
>     logging.basicConfig(
>         level=getattr(logging, level.upper(), logging.INFO),
>         format="%(asctime)s | %(levelname)s | %(name)s | %(message)s",
>     )
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `app/storage.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import mimetypes
> from pathlib import Path
> 
> import boto3
> from botocore.client import BaseClient
> 
> from app.config import Settings
> 
> 
> class ObjectStorage:
>     def __init__(self, settings: Settings) -> None:
>         session = boto3.session.Session()
>         self.bucket = settings.s3_bucket
>         self.client: BaseClient = session.client(
>             "s3",
>             endpoint_url=settings.s3_endpoint_url,
>             aws_access_key_id=settings.s3_access_key_id,
>             aws_secret_access_key=settings.s3_secret_access_key,
>             region_name=settings.s3_region,
>         )
> 
>     def download(self, key: str, local_path: Path) -> None:
>         local_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
>         self.client.download_file(self.bucket, key, str(local_path))
> 
>     def upload(self, local_path: Path, key: str, content_type: str | None = None) -> None:
>         content_type = content_type or mimetypes.guess_type(str(local_path))[0] or "application/octet-stream"
>         self.client.upload_file(
>             str(local_path),
>             self.bucket,
>             key,
>             ExtraArgs={"ContentType": content_type},
>         )
> 
>     def put_text(self, text: str, key: str) -> None:
>         self.client.put_object(
>             Bucket=self.bucket,
>             Key=key,
>             Body=text.encode("utf-8"),
>             ContentType="text/plain; charset=utf-8",
>         )
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `app/jobs.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import hashlib
> import json
> import logging
> from pathlib import Path
> 
> import psycopg
> from pypdf import PdfReader
> 
> from app.config import Settings
> from app.storage import ObjectStorage
> 
> logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
> 
> 
> def _safe_name(value: str) -> str:
>     return "".join(ch if ch.isalnum() or ch in ("-", "_", ".") else "_" for ch in value)
> 
> 
> def _sha256_file(path: Path) -> str:
>     h = hashlib.sha256()
>     with path.open("rb") as f:
>         for chunk in iter(lambda: f.read(1024 * 1024), b""):
>             h.update(chunk)
>     return h.hexdigest()
> 
> 
> def _extract_pdf_text(path: Path) -> str:
>     reader = PdfReader(str(path))
>     pages: list[str] = []
>     for i, page in enumerate(reader.pages, start=1):
>         try:
>             text = page.extract_text() or ""
>         except Exception as exc:
>             logger.warning("Failed text extraction on page %s: %s", i, exc)
>             text = ""
>         pages.append(f"\n\n--- PAGE {i} ---\n{text.strip()}")
>     return "".join(pages).strip()
> 
> 
> def _ensure_dirs(base: Path) -> dict[str, Path]:
>     paths = {
>         "tmp": base / "tmp",
>         "inbox": base / "inbox",
>         "ocr": base / "ocr",
>         "render": base / "render",
>         "logs": base / "logs",
>     }
>     for p in paths.values():
>         p.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
>     return paths
> 
> 
> def _update_file_asset(
>     database_url: str,
>     *,
>     file_asset_id: str,
>     sha256: str | None = None,
>     text_key: str | None = None,
>     extracted_text: str | None = None,
>     status: str = "processed",
>     error_message: str | None = None,
> ) -> None:
>     """
>     Assumes you already have a table like:
>       file_asset(id text primary key, status text, sha256 text, text_key text, extracted_text text, error_message text)
>     Adjust SQL if your schema names differ.
>     """
>     with psycopg.connect(database_url) as conn:
>         with conn.cursor() as cur:
>             cur.execute(
>                 """
>                 update file_asset
>                 set
>                     status = %s,
>                     sha256 = coalesce(%s, sha256),
>                     text_key = coalesce(%s, text_key),
>                     extracted_text = coalesce(%s, extracted_text),
>                     error_message = %s,
>                     updated_at = now()
>                 where id = %s
>                 """,
>                 (status, sha256, text_key, extracted_text, error_message, file_asset_id),
>             )
>         conn.commit()
> 
> 
> def ping(payload: dict) -> dict:
>     logger.info("Ping job received: %s", payload)
>     return {"ok": True, "payload": payload}
> 
> 
> def ingest_file(
>     *,
>     file_asset_id: str,
>     object_key: str,
>     original_filename: str,
>     mime_type: str | None = None,
> ) -> dict:
>     settings = Settings.from_env()
>     dirs = _ensure_dirs(Path(settings.workspace_dir))
>     storage = ObjectStorage(settings)
> 
>     safe_filename = _safe_name(original_filename)
>     local_path = dirs["inbox"] / safe_filename
> 
>     logger.info("Downloading %s to %s", object_key, local_path)
>     storage.download(object_key, local_path)
> 
>     sha256 = _sha256_file(local_path)
>     suffix = local_path.suffix.lower()
> 
>     extracted_text = ""
>     text_key = None
> 
>     if suffix == ".pdf":
>         logger.info("Extracting PDF text from %s", local_path)
>         extracted_text = _extract_pdf_text(local_path)
>         text_key = f"cases/fcfcu/derived/text/{local_path.stem}.txt"
>         storage.put_text(extracted_text, text_key)
>     elif suffix in {".txt", ".md", ".csv"}:
>         extracted_text = local_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore")
>         text_key = f"cases/fcfcu/derived/text/{local_path.stem}.txt"
>         storage.put_text(extracted_text, text_key)
>     else:
>         logger.info("No parser for %s yet. File kept as object only.", suffix)
> 
>     _update_file_asset(
>         settings.database_url,
>         file_asset_id=file_asset_id,
>         sha256=sha256,
>         text_key=text_key,
>         extracted_text=extracted_text[:50000] if extracted_text else None,
>         status="processed",
>         error_message=None,
>     )
> 
>     result = {
>         "ok": True,
>         "file_asset_id": file_asset_id,
>         "object_key": object_key,
>         "sha256": sha256,
>         "text_key": text_key,
>         "has_text": bool(extracted_text),
>     }
>     logger.info("Ingest complete: %s", json.dumps(result))
>     return result
> 
> 
> def mark_failed(file_asset_id: str, message: str) -> None:
>     settings = Settings.from_env()
>     _update_file_asset(
>         settings.database_url,
>         file_asset_id=file_asset_id,
>         status="failed",
>         error_message=message[:4000],
>     )
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `app/worker.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import logging
> import os
> import sys
> from pathlib import Path
> 
> from redis import Redis
> from rq import Connection, Queue, Worker
> 
> from app.config import Settings, configure_logging
> from app import jobs
> 
> logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
> 
> 
> def main() -> None:
>     settings = Settings.from_env()
>     configure_logging(settings.log_level)
> 
>     Path(settings.workspace_dir).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
> 
>     redis_conn = Redis.from_url(settings.redis_url)
>     queue_names = os.getenv("RQ_QUEUES", "default,ingest,ocr").split(",")
>     queue_names = [q.strip() for q in queue_names if q.strip()]
> 
>     logger.info("Starting worker")
>     logger.info("Queues: %s", queue_names)
>     logger.info("Workspace: %s", settings.workspace_dir)
> 
>     with Connection(redis_conn):
>         worker = Worker([Queue(name) for name in queue_names])
>         worker.work(with_scheduler=False)
> 
> 
> if __name__ == "__main__":
>     try:
>         main()
>     except KeyboardInterrupt:
>         logger.info("Worker stopped")
>         sys.exit(0)
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What your API should enqueue
> 
> From your FastAPI app, enqueue jobs like this:
> 
> ```python
> from redis import Redis
> from rq import Queue
> 
> redis_conn = Redis.from_url(os.environ["REDIS_URL"])
> q = Queue("ingest", connection=redis_conn)
> 
> job = q.enqueue(
>     "app.jobs.ingest_file",
>     file_asset_id=file_asset_id,
>     object_key=object_key,
>     original_filename=original_filename,
>     mime_type=mime_type,
> )
> ```
> 
> That means the **worker repo must be importable as `app.jobs.ingest_file`**, which the code above already is.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Minimum DB table this expects
> 
> Your worker assumes a table like this exists in Postgres:
> 
> ```sql
> create table if not exists file_asset (
>   id text primary key,
>   status text default 'pending',
>   sha256 text,
>   text_key text,
>   extracted_text text,
>   error_message text,
>   updated_at timestamptz default now()
> );
> ```
> 
> You can expand later with:
> - `case_id`
> - `source_path`
> - `source_type`
> - `mime_type`
> - `size_bytes`
> - `page_count`
> - `thumb_key`
> - `ocr_json_key`
> 
> But this gets you moving now.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Railway settings for the worker service
> 
> Set these:
> 
> ### Start command
> ```bash
> python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> ### Volume mount path
> ```text
> /workspace
> ```
> 
> ### Env vars
> Use shared refs from your Railway project:
> - `REDIS_URL`
> - `DATABASE_URL`
> - bucket creds:
>   - `S3_ENDPOINT_URL`
>   - `S3_ACCESS_KEY_ID`
>   - `S3_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`
>   - `S3_BUCKET`
>   - `S3_REGION`
> 
> And:
> - `WORKSPACE_DIR=/workspace`
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What this worker does right now
> 
> It already handles:
> - queue processing
> - downloading from bucket
> - hashing files
> - extracting text from PDFs
> - writing extracted text back to bucket
> - updating Postgres status
> 
> So this is not fake scaffolding. It’s actually usable.
> 
> ## What it does not do yet
> 
> Not yet:
> - OCR on image PDFs
> - OCR on JPG/PNG/HEIC
> - video transcription
> - thumbnail generation
> - embeddings
> 
> That’s phase 2, not phase 1.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Next move
> 
> Do this in order:
> 
> 1. create worker repo with these files  
> 2. deploy on Railway  
> 3. attach volume at `/workspace`  
> 4. set env vars  
> 5. make sure API and worker share the same Redis + Postgres + bucket  
> 6. enqueue one `ping` job, then one `ingest_file` job
> 
> If you want, next I’ll give you the matching **FastAPI enqueue endpoint code** for your Railway FastAPI repo so the API can send jobs to this worker cleanly.

### User
> {'asset_pointer': 'sediment://file_00000000818471f582990ab05e2bd974', 'content_type': 'image_asset_pointer', 'fovea': None, 'height': 701, 'metadata': {'asset_pointer_link': None, 'container_pixel_height': None, 'container_pixel_width': None, 'dalle': None, 'emu_omit_glimpse_image': None, 'emu_patches_override': None, 'generation': None, 'gizmo': None, 'is_no_auth_placeholder': None, 'lpe_delta_encoding_channel': None, 'lpe_keep_patch_ijhw': None, 'sanitized': True, 'watermarked_asset_pointer': None}, 'size_bytes': 75197, 'width': 692}yes, pleaee cause i was like, how do i enqueue one ping job then ingest, lol i aint even got no ui yet.

### Assistant
> Exactly. You need the API to expose a couple of dead-simple endpoints so you can hit them from Swagger/UI even before the frontend exists.
> 
> Here’s the matching FastAPI side.
> 
> ## What you’re building
> 
> Your API gets 3 starter endpoints:
> 
> - `POST /jobs/ping` → proves Redis + worker are talking
> - `POST /jobs/ingest` → enqueue one file ingest job
> - `GET /jobs/{job_id}` → check job status/result
> 
> That’s enough to test the whole pipe without a UI.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Add these dependencies to your **FastAPI repo**
> 
> If they are not already there:
> 
> ```txt
> redis==5.0.8
> rq==1.16.2
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Suggested FastAPI file layout
> 
> ```text
> app/
> ├─ main.py
> ├─ core/
> │  ├─ config.py
> │  └─ queue.py
> └─ api/
>    └─ jobs.py
> ```
> 
> If your template layout is a little different, no big deal. Just wire the imports accordingly.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `app/core/config.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import os
> from dataclasses import dataclass
> 
> 
> @dataclass(frozen=True)
> class Settings:
>     redis_url: str
> 
>     @staticmethod
>     def from_env() -> "Settings":
>         return Settings(
>             redis_url=os.environ["REDIS_URL"],
>         )
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `app/core/queue.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> from redis import Redis
> from rq import Queue
> 
> from app.core.config import Settings
> 
> 
> def get_redis_connection() -> Redis:
>     settings = Settings.from_env()
>     return Redis.from_url(settings.redis_url)
> 
> 
> def get_queue(name: str = "default") -> Queue:
>     return Queue(name, connection=get_redis_connection())
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## `app/api/jobs.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> from typing import Any
> 
> from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
> from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
> from rq.job import Job
> 
> from app.core.queue import get_queue, get_redis_connection
> 
> router = APIRouter(prefix="/jobs", tags=["jobs"])
> 
> 
> class PingRequest(BaseModel):
>     message: str = Field(default="hello from api")
> 
> 
> class IngestRequest(BaseModel):
>     file_asset_id: str
>     object_key: str
>     original_filename: str
>     mime_type: str | None = None
> 
> 
> @router.post("/ping")
> def enqueue_ping(payload: PingRequest) -> dict[str, Any]:
>     q = get_queue("default")
> 
>     job = q.enqueue(
>         "app.jobs.ping",
>         {"message": payload.message},
>         job_timeout="5m",
>     )
> 
>     return {
>         "ok": True,
>         "job_id": job.id,
>         "queue": "default",
>         "status": job.get_status(refresh=True),
>     }
> 
> 
> @router.post("/ingest")
> def enqueue_ingest(payload: IngestRequest) -> dict[str, Any]:
>     q = get_queue("ingest")
> 
>     job = q.enqueue(
>         "app.jobs.ingest_file",
>         file_asset_id=payload.file_asset_id,
>         object_key=payload.object_key,
>         original_filename=payload.original_filename,
>         mime_type=payload.mime_type,
>         job_timeout="30m",
>     )
> 
>     return {
>         "ok": True,
>         "job_id": job.id,
>         "queue": "ingest",
>         "status": job.get_status(refresh=True),
>         "file_asset_id": payload.file_asset_id,
>     }
> 
> 
> @router.get("/{job_id}")
> def get_job_status(job_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
>     redis_conn = get_redis_connection()
> 
>     try:
>         job = Job.fetch(job_id, connection=redis_conn)
>     except Exception as exc:
>         raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail=f"Job not found: {job_id}") from exc
> 
>     response: dict[str, Any] = {
>         "job_id": job.id,
>         "status": job.get_status(refresh=True),
>         "created_at": job.created_at.isoformat() if job.created_at else None,
>         "enqueued_at": job.enqueued_at.isoformat() if job.enqueued_at else None,
>         "ended_at": job.ended_at.isoformat() if job.ended_at else None,
>     }
> 
>     if job.is_finished:
>         response["result"] = job.result
>     if job.is_failed:
>         response["error"] = job.exc_info
> 
>     return response
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Wire it into `app/main.py`
> 
> If your current `main.py` is basic, use this pattern:
> 
> ```python
> from fastapi import FastAPI
> from app.api.jobs import router as jobs_router
> 
> app = FastAPI(title="FCFCU API")
> 
> app.include_router(jobs_router)
> 
> 
> @app.get("/")
> def root():
>     return {"ok": True, "service": "fcfcu-api"}
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Important reality check
> 
> Right now your API enqueues strings like:
> 
> ```python
> "app.jobs.ping"
> "app.jobs.ingest_file"
> ```
> 
> That means the **worker service must contain those exact functions** in a module importable as `app.jobs`.
> 
> Which it does, based on the worker code I gave you.
> 
> So:
> - API repo does **not** need the worker logic
> - Worker repo does **need** `app/jobs.py`
> 
> That separation is correct.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## How to test with no frontend
> 
> Once deployed, open your FastAPI docs:
> 
> ```text
> https://your-api-url/docs
> ```
> 
> Then do this:
> 
> ### 1. Test ping
> Run `POST /jobs/ping` with:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "message": "test ping from swagger"
> }
> ```
> 
> You should get back:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "ok": true,
>   "job_id": "...",
>   "queue": "default",
>   "status": "queued"
> }
> ```
> 
> Copy the `job_id`.
> 
> ### 2. Check job
> Run `GET /jobs/{job_id}`
> 
> If all is working, you’ll see:
> - `queued`
> - then `started`
> - then `finished`
> 
> And eventually a result like:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "job_id": "...",
>   "status": "finished",
>   "result": {
>     "ok": true,
>     "payload": {
>       "message": "test ping from swagger"
>     }
>   }
> }
> ```
> 
> ### 3. Test ingest
> Then run `POST /jobs/ingest` with something like:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "file_asset_id": "FILE-0001",
>   "object_key": "cases/fcfcu/inbox/test.pdf",
>   "original_filename": "test.pdf",
>   "mime_type": "application/pdf"
> }
> ```
> 
> That assumes:
> - the file already exists in your bucket at `cases/fcfcu/inbox/test.pdf`
> - and `FILE-0001` already exists in your `file_asset` table
> 
> If not, it’ll enqueue fine but fail in worker execution.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What you need in Noco/Postgres right now
> 
> At minimum, create one record in your backing table for testing.
> 
> If your underlying Postgres has this table:
> 
> ```sql
> create table if not exists file_asset (
>   id text primary key,
>   status text default 'pending',
>   sha256 text,
>   text_key text,
>   extracted_text text,
>   error_message text,
>   updated_at timestamptz default now()
> );
> ```
> 
> Then insert one test row:
> 
> ```sql
> insert into file_asset (id, status)
> values ('FILE-0001', 'pending')
> on conflict (id) do nothing;
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Best next test sequence
> 
> Do this in order:
> 
> 1. deploy API code
> 2. deploy worker code
> 3. confirm both share same `REDIS_URL`
> 4. use `/docs` to hit `POST /jobs/ping`
> 5. use `GET /jobs/{job_id}` to confirm worker picked it up
> 6. upload one test PDF into bucket
> 7. insert one `file_asset` row
> 8. hit `POST /jobs/ingest`
> 
> That gets you a real end-to-end lane.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Tiny but important fix
> 
> Your worker box showing **Completed** instead of **Online** usually means Railway ran it like a one-off command and it exited, or the process crashed and didn’t stay attached as a long-running service.
> 
> For the worker service, verify:
> 
> ### Start command
> ```bash
> python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> ### Root directory
> the repo root of the worker project
> 
> ### Procfile
> optional, but fine:
> ```txt
> worker: python -m app.worker
> ```
> 
> If it still shows completed, the service is not running as a persistent service yet.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## IDE prompt for your FastAPI repo
> 
> Paste this into the IDE for the API repo:
> 
> ```text
> Add Redis/RQ-backed job enqueue endpoints to this FastAPI app.
> 
> Requirements:
> 1. Add app/core/config.py with a Settings dataclass reading REDIS_URL from env.
> 2. Add app/core/queue.py with:
>    - get_redis_connection()
>    - get_queue(name: str = "default")
> 3. Add app/api/jobs.py with a FastAPI APIRouter(prefix="/jobs", tags=["jobs"]) containing:
>    - POST /jobs/ping
>      - accepts JSON body { "message": "..." }
>      - enqueues job "app.jobs.ping" onto queue "default"
>      - returns ok, job_id, queue, status
>    - POST /jobs/ingest
>      - accepts JSON body:
>        {
>          "file_asset_id": str,
>          "object_key": str,
>          "original_filename": str,
>          "mime_type": Optional[str]
>        }
>      - enqueues job "app.jobs.ingest_file" onto queue "ingest"
>      - returns ok, job_id, queue, status, file_asset_id
>    - GET /jobs/{job_id}
>      - fetches RQ job by id
>      - returns job_id, status, created_at, enqueued_at, ended_at
>      - includes result if finished
>      - includes error if failed
> 4. Update app/main.py to include the jobs router.
> 5. Add required dependencies for redis and rq.
> 6. Do not add frontend code.
> 7. Keep code minimal, production-clean, and import-safe.
> 
> Assume the worker lives in a separate Railway service and exposes functions by string path:
> - app.jobs.ping
> - app.jobs.ingest_file
> 
> Return all changed files.
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What you can do immediately in Railway
> 
> While IDE cooks:
> - make sure API has `REDIS_URL`
> - make sure worker has `REDIS_URL`, `DATABASE_URL`, bucket vars, and `WORKSPACE_DIR=/workspace`
> - make sure worker start command is `python -m app.worker`
> - upload one tiny test PDF into the bucket
> 
> Next thing I’d do after this is give you the **exact SQL/Noco schema for `file_asset` + `case_file` + `chunk`** so your ingest lane isn’t half-assed.

### User
> what fast api docs? ```markdown 
> . 📂 FastAPI
> ├── 📄 README.md
> └── 📂 api/
> │  ├── 📄 jobs.py
> └── 📂 core/
> │  ├── 📄 config.py
> │  ├── 📄 queue.py
> └── 📄 main.py
> ```

### Assistant
> The built-in Swagger page. FastAPI auto-generates it.
> 
> If your app is running, you should have:
> 
> - `/docs` → Swagger UI
> - `/redoc` → ReDoc
> - `/openapi.json` → raw schema
> 
> So if Railway gives you something like:
> 
> ```text
> https://fcfcu-api-production.up.railway.app
> ```
> 
> then the docs are:
> 
> ```text
> https://fcfcu-api-production.up.railway.app/docs
> ```
> 
> ## Your actual problem
> 
> Your tree is not laid out like the code I gave earlier assumed.
> 
> You have:
> 
> ```text
> FastAPI/
> ├── main.py
> ├── api/jobs.py
> └── core/config.py
>    core/queue.py
> ```
> 
> So your imports should be from `api...` and `core...`, **not** `app.api...` or `app.core...`.
> 
> That means your API code needs to match **your repo layout**, not the generic example.
> 
> ## Use this version instead
> 
> ### `main.py`
> 
> ```python
> from fastapi import FastAPI
> from api.jobs import router as jobs_router
> 
> app = FastAPI(title="FCFCU API")
> 
> app.include_router(jobs_router)
> 
> 
> @app.get("/")
> def root():
>     return {"ok": True, "service": "fcfcu-api"}
> ```
> 
> ### `core/config.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import os
> from dataclasses import dataclass
> 
> 
> @dataclass(frozen=True)
> class Settings:
>     redis_url: str
> 
>     @staticmethod
>     def from_env() -> "Settings":
>         return Settings(
>             redis_url=os.environ["REDIS_URL"],
>         )
> ```
> 
> ### `core/queue.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> from redis import Redis
> from rq import Queue
> 
> from core.config import Settings
> 
> 
> def get_redis_connection() -> Redis:
>     settings = Settings.from_env()
>     return Redis.from_url(settings.redis_url)
> 
> 
> def get_queue(name: str = "default") -> Queue:
>     return Queue(name, connection=get_redis_connection())
> ```
> 
> ### `api/jobs.py`
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> from typing import Any
> 
> from fastapi import APIRouter, HTTPException
> from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
> from rq.job import Job
> 
> from core.queue import get_queue, get_redis_connection
> 
> router = APIRouter(prefix="/jobs", tags=["jobs"])
> 
> 
> class PingRequest(BaseModel):
>     message: str = Field(default="hello from api")
> 
> 
> class IngestRequest(BaseModel):
>     file_asset_id: str
>     object_key: str
>     original_filename: str
>     mime_type: str | None = None
> 
> 
> @router.post("/ping")
> def enqueue_ping(payload: PingRequest) -> dict[str, Any]:
>     q = get_queue("default")
> 
>     job = q.enqueue(
>         "app.jobs.ping",
>         {"message": payload.message},
>         job_timeout="5m",
>     )
> 
>     return {
>         "ok": True,
>         "job_id": job.id,
>         "queue": "default",
>         "status": job.get_status(refresh=True),
>     }
> 
> 
> @router.post("/ingest")
> def enqueue_ingest(payload: IngestRequest) -> dict[str, Any]:
>     q = get_queue("ingest")
> 
>     job = q.enqueue(
>         "app.jobs.ingest_file",
>         file_asset_id=payload.file_asset_id,
>         object_key=payload.object_key,
>         original_filename=payload.original_filename,
>         mime_type=payload.mime_type,
>         job_timeout="30m",
>     )
> 
>     return {
>         "ok": True,
>         "job_id": job.id,
>         "queue": "ingest",
>         "status": job.get_status(refresh=True),
>         "file_asset_id": payload.file_asset_id,
>     }
> 
> 
> @router.get("/{job_id}")
> def get_job_status(job_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
>     redis_conn = get_redis_connection()
> 
>     try:
>         job = Job.fetch(job_id, connection=redis_conn)
>     except Exception as exc:
>         raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail=f"Job not found: {job_id}") from exc
> 
>     response: dict[str, Any] = {
>         "job_id": job.id,
>         "status": job.get_status(refresh=True),
>         "created_at": job.created_at.isoformat() if job.created_at else None,
>         "enqueued_at": job.enqueued_at.isoformat() if job.enqueued_at else None,
>         "ended_at": job.ended_at.isoformat() if job.ended_at else None,
>     }
> 
>     if job.is_finished:
>         response["result"] = job.result
>     if job.is_failed:
>         response["error"] = job.exc_info
> 
>     return response
> ```
> 
> ## Railway start command
> 
> For this repo, use:
> 
> ```bash
> uvicorn main:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port $PORT
> ```
>

### Assistant
> Good. That’s the right move.
> 
> Do **not** try to reorganize structure and normalize names and migrate metadata all at once. That’s how repos turn into soup.
> 
> Here’s the order:
> 
> ## Right now
> Finish **filename normalization only** first.
> 
> Rules:
> - no broken links
> - no content edits unless required
> - no folder moves during this pass
> - no frontmatter rewrites during this pass
> - no database sync during this pass
> 
> The goal is one clean naming layer.
> 
> ## Then do this next
> Run a **fresh manifest export** after the rename pass finishes.
> 
> You want:
> - current path
> - current filename
> - extension
> - parent folder
> - relative path
> - size
> - modified time
> - guessed record type
> - guessed phase
> - guessed lane
> - any extracted ids like `D####`, `T-####`, `ISS-####`, `EX-V-...`
> 
> That manifest becomes the source of truth for the next pass.
> 
> ## Then do a triage pass
> Split everything into 4 buckets:
> 
> 1. **Good**
>    already named correctly, stays put
> 
> 2. **Needs metadata only**
>    file is named fine, but frontmatter/db row is incomplete
> 
> 3. **Needs relocation**
>    file name is fine enough, but wrong folder
> 
> 4. **Quarantine**
>    duplicates, junk, autosaves, broken exports, random garbage names
> 
> That prevents you from over-touching files that are already fine.
> 
> ## Then do structure moves
> Only after the manifest is rebuilt.
> 
> Move by rule, not by vibes:
> - court-filed docs → `03_Pleadings`
> - raw evidence → `1_Evidence`
> - working notes / strategy / prep → `0_Case_Ops`
> - machine outputs / csv / manifests → `2_Data`
> - reports / analysis → `3_reports`
> - rules / schema / repo docs → `4_Governance`
> - scripts only → `5_Scripts`
> - dead junk only → `9_Archive`
> 
> ## Then do database sync
> Once names and locations are stable:
> - create/update rows from manifest
> - map markdown frontmatter into fields
> - use markdown body as `body/content/notes`
> - do not let the DB become the thing that decides filenames yet
> 
> Filesystem first. DB second. Otherwise you’ll be reconciling a moving target like an idiot.
> 
> ## What you should have the AI do now
> Have it produce these 3 outputs after normalization:
> 
> ### 1. rename_log.csv
> Columns:
> - old_relative_path
> - new_relative_path
> - action
> - reason
> - status
> 
> ### 2. unresolved_items.csv
> Anything it could not confidently rename:
> - path
> - guessed_type
> - guessed_reason
> - recommended_action
> 
> ### 3. content_manifest_post_normalize.csv
> Fresh full manifest of current state
> 
> ## Hard rule
> Anything with legal significance gets treated cautiously:
> - filed pleadings
> - court orders
> - exhibit files
> - transcript files
> - certified mail proof
> - service proof
> - notices
> - demand letters
> 
> For those, normalize carefully and preserve document identity. Don’t get cute and rewrite names so hard that you lose traceability.
> 
> ## Best next move
> Tell the AI:
> 
> ```text
> Complete filename normalization only. Do not move folders. Do not rewrite document bodies. Do not update frontmatter except where absolutely required to preserve filename-derived IDs. Generate:
> 1. rename_log.csv
> 2. unresolved_items.csv
> 3. content_manifest_post_normalize.csv
> 
> Use current repo state as source of truth. Preserve legal traceability for pleadings, exhibits, orders, notices, transcripts, and service documents.
> ```
> 
> Then send me the new manifest or even just the summary counts, and I’ll help you design the **move rules** cleanly instead of letting this thing freestyle your case repo into hell.
