### Assistant
> Yes. That is exactly how to handle it.
> 
> Do **not** read these one by one first. That’s a waste of life.
> 
> What you need is a **draft block extractor** that scans every markdown file and outputs a CSV of the internal chunks so you can see:
> 
> - which files are really just one record
> - which files are multi-entry containers
> - which files are templates
> - which files are summaries only
> - which files can be split into rows
> - which files should stay documents
> 
> ## The move
> 
> Treat each `.md` as one of these 5 types:
> 
> 1. **single_record**  
>    one file = one row
> 
> 2. **multi_record**  
>    one file contains many entries that should become many rows
> 
> 3. **template**  
>    reusable form, should not become case data rows
> 
> 4. **packet_manifest**  
>    list of exhibits/docs/tasks/attachments
> 
> 5. **narrative_summary**  
>    analysis/memo/brief that should stay a document, maybe with one parent row only
> 
> ## What to extract first
> 
> From every markdown file, pull:
> 
> - relative path
> - filename
> - top-level heading
> - frontmatter keys
> - frontmatter values
> - number of headings
> - heading hierarchy
> - number of bullet items
> - number of numbered items
> - number of checklist items
> - number of tables
> - first 300 chars of body
> - repeated pattern signals:
>   - `EX-`
>   - `ISS-`
>   - `D-`
>   - `T-`
>   - dates
>   - agency names
>   - “Exhibit”
>   - “Violation”
>   - “Count”
>   - “Claim”
>   - “Task”
>   - “Action”
>   - “Deadline”
> 
> That gives you a structural inventory without reading everything.
> 
> ## The key idea
> 
> You need **two outputs**, not one:
> 
> ### 1. file-level inventory
> One row per markdown file.
> 
> Use this to classify the file.
> 
> ### 2. block-level inventory
> One row per extracted block inside a markdown file.
> 
> Use this to decide whether that file should be split into rows.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Heuristic for “this file probably contains multiple records”
> 
> Usually true if:
> - 3+ H2 headings
> - lots of repeated H3/H4 sections
> - many bullets under repeating headers
> - repeated IDs like `ISS-`, `T-`, `EX-`
> - multiple dated subsections
> - a markdown table with many rows
> - section titles like:
>   - “Violations”
>   - “Exhibits”
>   - “Tasks”
>   - “Timeline”
>   - “Action Items”
>   - “Counts”
>   - “Authorities”
> 
> Usually **not** a multi-record file if:
> - affidavit
> - narrative
> - memo
> - brief
> - declaration
> - statement
> - summary
> - draft letter
> - template
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Best target tables from this `_drafts` tree
> 
> Based on your tree, I’d map like this:
> 
> - `affidavits/` → mostly **documents table**, maybe one row each
> - `complaint/*/*Violation_Excerpt*.md` → likely **claims/issues/evidence excerpt rows**
> - `complaint/*/*Narrative*.md` → usually **document row**, maybe linked to agency complaint row
> - `manifest/` → likely **manifest_items / exhibit_rows / filing_packet_rows**
> - `outreach/` → **contacts/outreach drafts/documents**
> - `packets/` → usually **packet rows + packet items**
> - `plan/` → **tasks / phases / strategy rows**
> - `summary/` → mostly **document rows**, but some may contain structured lists worth extracting
> - `templates/` → **template library**, not case facts
> 
> So yes, a lot of this absolutely wants to become rows.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Fastest way to get control
> 
> Run a scanner that:
> - parses YAML frontmatter
> - splits markdown into heading blocks
> - creates:
>   - `draft_file_inventory.csv`
>   - `draft_block_inventory.csv`
>   - `draft_extraction_candidates.csv`
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Suggested block splitting rule
> 
> Split on:
> - H1 / H2 / H3 headings
> - markdown tables
> - bullet groups
> - checklist groups
> 
> Each extracted block gets:
> - source file
> - block path like `H1 > H2 > H3`
> - heading text
> - block type
> - block text preview
> - probable entity type
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Python script
> 
> This will do the first useful pass.
> 
> ```python
> from __future__ import annotations
> 
> import csv
> import re
> from pathlib import Path
> from collections import Counter
> 
> ROOT = Path(r"g:\My Drive\QiVault\3_projects\3.3_cases\30.06_CASE_FCFCU\content\0_Case_Ops\_drafts")
> OUT_DIR = ROOT.parent / "_draft_analysis"
> 
> OUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
> 
> MD_GLOB = "*.md"
> 
> ID_PATTERNS = {
>     "issue_ids": r"\bISS-[A-Z0-9\-]+\b",
>     "task_ids": r"\bT-\d{3,6}\b",
>     "doc_ids": r"\bD-\d{3,6}\b",
>     "exhibit_ids": r"\bEX-[A-Z0-9\-]+\b",
>     "dates": r"\b\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}\b",
> }
> 
> AGENCY_TERMS = [
>     "CFPB", "NCUA", "HUD", "DOJ", "Indiana AG", "DFI", "ICRC",
>     "Equifax", "Experian", "TransUnion", "FCC", "SSA", "Medicaid"
> ]
> 
> def parse_frontmatter(text: str):
>     if not text.startswith("---\n"):
>         return {}, text
>     parts = text.split("\n---\n", 1)
>     if len(parts) != 2:
>         return {}, text
>     raw_fm = parts[0][4:]
>     body = parts[1]
>     fm = {}
>     for line in raw_fm.splitlines():
>         if ":" in line:
>             k, v = line.split(":", 1)
>             fm[k.strip()] = v.strip()
>     return fm, body
> 
> def count_regex(pattern: str, text: str) -> int:
>     return len(re.findall(pattern, text, flags=re.IGNORECASE | re.MULTILINE))
> 
> def find_terms(terms: list[str], text: str) -> list[str]:
>     found = []
>     low = text.lower()
>     for term in terms:
>         if term.lower() in low:
>             found.append(term)
>     return found
> 
> def split_heading_blocks(body: str):
>     lines = body.splitlines()
>     blocks = []
>     current = {
>         "heading_level": 0,
>         "heading": "_ROOT",
>         "path": "_ROOT",
>         "content_lines": []
>     }
>     heading_stack = []
> 
>     heading_re = re.compile(r"^(#{1,6})\s+(.*)$")
> 
>     for line in lines:
>         m = heading_re.match(line)
>         if m:
>             # flush old block
>             if current["content_lines"] or current["heading"] != "_ROOT":
>                 blocks.append(current)
> 
>             level = len(m.group(1))
>             heading = m.group(2).strip()
> 
>             heading_stack = [h for h in heading_stack if h["level"] < level]
>             heading_stack.append({"level": level, "heading": heading})
>             path = " > ".join(x["heading"] for x in heading_stack)
> 
>             current = {
>                 "heading_level": level,
>                 "heading": heading,
>                 "path": path,
>                 "content_lines": []
>             }
>         else:
>             current["content_lines"].append(line)
> 
>     if current["content_lines"] or current["heading"] != "_ROOT":
>         blocks.append(current)
> 
>     return blocks
> 
> def classify_file(path: Path, body: str, frontmatter: dict, blocks: list[dict]) -> str:
>     rel = str(path.relative_to(ROOT)).lower()
>     name = path.name.lower()
> 
>     if "template" in rel or rel.startswith("templates") or name.startswith("tpl_"):
>         return "template"
>     if "manifest" in rel:
>         return "packet_manifest"
>     if any(x in rel for x in ["affidavit", "declaration", "brief", "memo", "summary"]):
>         return "narrative_summary"
> 
>     h2_count = sum(1 for b in blocks if b["heading_level"] == 2)
>     bullet_count = count_regex(r"^\s*[-*]\s+", body)
>     numbered_count = count_regex(r"^\s*\d+\.\s+", body)
>     table_count = count_regex(r"^\|.+\|\s*$", body)
> 
>     repeated_ids = sum(count_regex(pat, body) for pat in ID_PATTERNS.values())
> 
>     if h2_count >= 3 or bullet_count >= 8 or numbered_count >= 8 or table_count >= 4 or repeated_ids >= 8:
>         return "multi_record"
> 
>     return "single_record"
> 
> def infer_block_type(text: str) -> str:
>     stripped = text.strip()
>     if not stripped:
>         return "empty"
>     if re.search(r"^\|.+\|\s*$", stripped, flags=re.MULTILINE):
>         return "table"
>     if re.search(r"^\s*[-*]\s+", stripped, flags=re.MULTILINE):
>         return "bullet_list"
>     if re.search(r"^\s*\d+\.\s+", stripped, flags=re.MULTILINE):
>         return "numbered_list"
>     if re.search(r"^\s*[-*]\s+\[[ xX]\]\s+", stripped, flags=re.MULTILINE):
>         return "checklist"
>     return "paragraph"
> 
> def probable_entity_type(path: Path, heading: str, text: str) -> str:
>     sample = f"{path} {heading} {text[:800]}".lower()
> 
>     if "violation" in sample or "issue" in sample or "claim" in sample:
>         return "issue_or_claim"
>     if "task" in sample or "action" in sample or "follow-up" in sample or "follow up" in sample:
>         return "task"
>     if "exhibit" in sample or "attachment" in sample or "evidence" in sample:
>         return "exhibit_or_evidence"
>     if "timeline" in sample or "event" in sample or re.search(r"\b20\d{2}-\d{2}-\d{2}\b", sample):
>         return "event"
>     if "contact" in sample or "attorney" in sample or "advocate" in sample or "outreach" in sample:
>         return "contact_or_outreach"
>     if "packet" in sample or "manifest" in sample:
>         return "packet_item"
>     return "document_text"
> 
> file_rows = []
> block_rows = []
> candidate_rows = []
> 
> for path in ROOT.rglob(MD_GLOB):
>     text = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8", errors="ignore")
>     frontmatter, body = parse_frontmatter(text)
>     blocks = split_heading_blocks(body)
> 
>     rel = str(path.relative_to(ROOT))
>     body_preview = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", body.strip())[:300]
>     agencies = find_terms(AGENCY_TERMS, body)
> 
>     counts = {key: count_regex(pat, body) for key, pat in ID_PATTERNS.items()}
>     heading_count = sum(1 for b in blocks if b["heading"] != "_ROOT")
> 
>     bullet_count = count_regex(r"^\s*[-*]\s+", body)
>     numbered_count = count_regex(r"^\s*\d+\.\s+", body)
>     checklist_count = count_regex(r"^\s*[-*]\s+\[[ xX]\]\s+", body)
>     table_line_count = count_regex(r"^\|.+\|\s*$", body)
> 
>     classification = classify_file(path, body, frontmatter, blocks)
> 
>     file_rows.append({
>         "relative_path": rel,
>         "filename": path.name,
>         "parent_folder": path.parent.name,
>         "frontmatter_keys": ", ".join(frontmatter.keys()),
>         "frontmatter_count": len(frontmatter),
>         "heading_count": heading_count,
>         "block_count": len(blocks),
>         "bullet_count": bullet_count,
>         "numbered_count": numbered_count,
>         "checklist_count": checklist_count,
>         "table_line_count": table_line_count,
>         "issue_id_count": counts["issue_ids"],
>         "task_id_count": counts["task_ids"],
>         "doc_id_count": counts["doc_ids"],
>         "exhibit_id_count": counts["exhibit_ids"],
>         "date_count": counts["dates"],
>         "agencies_found": ", ".join(agencies),
>         "classification": classification,
>         "body_preview": body_preview,
>     })
> 
>     for idx, block in enumerate(blocks, start=1):
>         block_text = "\n".join(block["content_lines"]).strip()
>         preview = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", block_text)[:250]
>         block_type = infer_block_type(block_text)
>         entity_type = probable_entity_type(path, block["heading"], block_text)
> 
>         block_rows.append({
>             "relative_path": rel,
>             "block_num": idx,
>             "heading_level": block["heading_level"],
>             "heading": block["heading"],
>             "heading_path": block["path"],
>             "block_type": block_type,
>             "entity_type": entity_type,
>             "char_count": len(block_text),
>             "line_count": len(block["content_lines"]),
>             "issue_id_count": count_regex(ID_PATTERNS["issue_ids"], block_text),
>             "task_id_count": count_regex(ID_PATTERNS["task_ids"], block_text),
>             "doc_id_count": count_regex(ID_PATTERNS["doc_ids"], block_text),
>             "exhibit_id_count": count_regex(ID_PATTERNS["exhibit_ids"], block_text),
>             "date_count": count_regex(ID_PATTERNS["dates"], block_text),
>             "preview": preview,
>         })
> 
>         score = 0
>         if block_type in {"table", "bullet_list", "numbered_list", "checklist"}:
>             score += 2
>         if entity_type in {"issue_or_claim", "task", "exhibit_or_evidence", "event", "packet_item"}:
>             score += 2
>         if len(block_text) > 150:
>             score += 1
>         if count_regex(ID_PATTERNS["dates"], block_text) > 0:
>             score += 1
> 
>         if score >= 4:
>             candidate_rows.append({
>                 "relative_path": rel,
>                 "block_num": idx,
>                 "heading_path": block["path"],
>                 "block_type": block_type,
>                 "entity_type": entity_type,
>                 "candidate_score": score,
>                 "preview": preview,
>             })
> 
> def write_csv(path: Path, rows: list[dict]):
>     if not rows:
>         return
>     with path.open("w", newline="", encoding="utf-8-sig") as f:
>         writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=list(rows[0].keys()))
>         writer.writeheader()
>         writer.writerows(rows)
> 
> write_csv(OUT_DIR / "draft_file_inventory.csv", file_rows)
> write_csv(OUT_DIR / "draft_block_inventory.csv", block_rows)
> write_csv(OUT_DIR / "draft_extraction_candidates.csv", candidate_rows)
> 
> summary = Counter(r["classification"] for r in file_rows)
> print("Done.")
> print("Output folder:", OUT_DIR)
> print("File classifications:", dict(summary))
> print("Total markdown files:", len(file_rows))
> print("Total blocks:", len(block_rows))
> print("Extraction candidates:", len(candidate_rows))
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> ## What this gives you
> 
> ### `draft_file_inventory.csv`
> Use this to sort files by likely conversion type.
> 
> ### `draft_block_inventory.csv`
> Use this to inspect blocks without opening every file.
> 
> ### `draft_extraction_candidates.csv`
> Your shortlist of blocks that probably deserve conversion into rows.
> 
> ---
> 
> ## Then what?
> 
> After that, do a second pass that maps likely files into extraction strategies:
> 
> - `Violation_Excerpt` files → split into issue/claim/evidence rows
> - `MANIFEST_*` files → split into packet item rows
> - `Talking_Points` / plan docs → split into tasks/strategy rows
> - trackers/checklists → split into checklist/task rows
> - affidavits/summaries/templates → leave as document rows
> 
> ---
> 
> ## My blunt recommendation
> 
> Do **not** try to directly convert all `_drafts` markdown into DB rows in one shot.
> 
> Do this in phases:
> 
> ### Phase 1
> Inventory and classify
> 
> ### Phase 2
> Extract only obvious structured files:
> - manifests
> - trackers
> - task lists
> - violation inventories
> - exhibit lists
> 
> ### Phase 3
> Leave narrative docs as documents with metadata + linked body
> 
> That keeps you from shredding legal prose into nonsense rows.
> 
> ## Best next move
> Run that extractor on `_drafts`, then send me:
> - the classification counts
> - 10–20 sample rows from `draft_extraction_candidates.csv`
> 
> Then I’ll tell you exactly which folders should become:
> - `tasks`
> - `issues`
> - `exhibits`
> - `complaints`
> - `packets`
> - `documents`
> - `strategy_rows`
> 
> and which ones should stay markdown.

> {'asset_pointer': 'sediment://file_0000000073b471fd9df4479e80bece6e', 'content_type': 'image_asset_pointer', 'fovea': None, 'height': 682, 'metadata': {'asset_pointer_link': None, 'container_pixel_height': None, 'container_pixel_width': None, 'dalle': None, 'emu_omit_glimpse_image': None, 'emu_patches_override': None, 'generation': None, 'gizmo': None, 'is_no_auth_placeholder': None, 'lpe_delta_encoding_channel': None, 'lpe_keep_patch_ijhw': None, 'sanitized': True, 'watermarked_asset_pointer': None}, 'size_bytes': 89390, 'width': 1227}WHAT NOW.

### Assistant
> Now you stop building random pieces and make one clean vertical slice work.
> 
> Your stack is basically assembled. The next move is not “more services.” It is:
> 
> **upload file → create DB row → enqueue job → worker processes file → results show in UI**
> 
> That’s the whole game.
> 
> ### What to do next
> 
> #### 1. Freeze the infra
> Do not add anything else right now.
> 
> You already have enough:
> - `fcfcu-web`
> - `fcfcu-api`
> - `fcfcu-worker`
> - `fcfcu-nocodb`
> - `postgres`
> - `fcfcu-redis`
> - `fcfcu-files`
> - optional `neo4j`
> 
> That’s plenty. The two ingestion-looking services are a smell unless one is a cron and one is a one-off. If they are duplicates, kill one.
> 
> ---
> 
> #### 2. Standardize the shared volume path
> Use the same mount path everywhere the files volume is attached.
> 
> Use this:
> 
> ```text
> /data/files
> ```
> 
> So:
> - API mount `fcfcu-files` at `/data/files`
> - Worker mount `fcfcu-files` at `/data/files`
> 
> Do **not** invent different paths per service. That is how people waste six hours for no reason.
> 
> Recommended internal folders on that volume:
> 
> ```text
> /data/files/originals
> /data/files/derived
> /data/files/ocr
> /data/files/thumbs
> /data/files/tmp
> ```
> 
> ---
> 
> #### 3. Set the minimum environment variables
> Make sure these exist.
> 
> **API**
> ```env
> DATABASE_URL=postgresql://...
> REDIS_URL=redis://...
> FILES_ROOT=/data/files
> UPLOAD_DIR=/data/files/originals
> DERIVED_DIR=/data/files/derived
> OCR_DIR=/data/files/ocr
> TMP_DIR=/data/files/tmp
> NOCO_BASE_URL=https://your-noco-url
> NOCO_API_TOKEN=...
> ```
> 
> **Worker**
> ```env
> DATABASE_URL=postgresql://...
> REDIS_URL=redis://...
> FILES_ROOT=/data/files
> UPLOAD_DIR=/data/files/originals
> DERIVED_DIR=/data/files/derived
> OCR_DIR=/data/files/ocr
> TMP_DIR=/data/files/tmp
> WORKER_POLL_INTERVAL=2
> ```
> 
> **Web**
> ```env
> VITE_API_BASE_URL=https://your-api-url
> ```
> 
> If the worker does not need its own volume, remove `fcfcu-worker-volume`. The important one is the shared `fcfcu-files` volume.
> 
> ---
> 
> #### 4. Define the first database model
> Do not start with all case tables. Start with one boring but critical table:
> 
> **documents**
> 
> Minimum columns:
> - `id`
> - `title`
> - `original_filename`
> - `stored_path`
> - `mime_type`
> - `status`
> - `source_type`
> - `sha256`
> - `size_bytes`
> - `ocr_text`
> - `summary`
> - `tags`
> - `created_at`
> - `updated_at`
> 
> Status values:
> - `uploaded`
> - `queued`
> - `processing`
> - `completed`
> - `failed`
> 
> That’s enough to prove the pipeline.
> 
> ---
> 
> #### 5. Make the API do only 3 useful things first
> Your FastAPI service needs these endpoints before anything fancy:
> 
> ```text
> POST /documents/upload
> GET  /documents
> GET  /documents/{id}
> POST /jobs/ingest/{id}
> GET  /health
> ```
> 
> What they should do:
> 
> **POST /documents/upload**
> - accept file upload
> - save file to `/data/files/originals/...`
> - insert document row in Postgres
> - set status = `uploaded`
> - optionally enqueue job immediately
> 
> **POST /jobs/ingest/{id}**
> - push a Redis job like:
> ```json
> {"type":"ingest_document","document_id":"..."}
> ```
> 
> **GET /documents**
> - list rows so the UI can show something real
> 
> ---
> 
> #### 6. Make the worker do one job only
> Do not make it “smart” yet.
> 
> Worker loop:
> - pop job from Redis
> - fetch document row
> - mark `processing`
> - inspect file type
> - if PDF/image:
>   - extract text / OCR if available
>   - save derived text under `/data/files/ocr/...`
> - update row with extracted text
> - mark `completed`
> - on failure mark `failed`
> 
> That’s it.
> 
> No graph logic.
> No fancy multi-stage orchestration.
> No embeddings yet.
> 
> ---
> 
> #### 7. Do one manual test end to end
> You need one proof run:
> 
> 1. Upload one PDF through API
> 2. Confirm file exists in `/data/files/originals`
> 3. Confirm row exists in Postgres/Noco
> 4. Enqueue ingest job
> 5. Worker picks it up
> 6. OCR/text gets saved
> 7. Row updates to `completed`
> 8. UI shows the row and extracted text
> 
> Until this works, everything else is cosplay.
> 
> ---
>

>

> 2. **Noco shows/edits document metadata**
> 3. **UI can browse/search docs**

>

> You were drifting toward infrastructure hoarding. The screenshot proves you are at the point where adding more architecture would be dumb. You have enough. Now force a single happy path into existence.
